confirmed_bugs.title: `code-dev new` dead-routes to the v1 scaffolder; the v4 scaffolder (code-dev-new) is unreachable
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The router has TWO branches for the same token. Line 76 `IF cmd ≡ "new" → EXEC(code-dev-init)` and line 92 `IF cmd ≡ "new" → EXEC(code-dev-new)`. The interpreter is first-match-wins — this is stated authoritatively in this same file at line 181 ("the interpreter takes the first match"), which is the basis of the F33 fix. So `new` always hits line 76 (code-dev-init) and line 92 (code-dev-new) is DEAD. The two targets are NOT equivalent: code-dev-init (lines 71-81) writes a v1 project (`phase: 1-study`, NO `schema-version`, NO `workflow-step`, NO `phases/` dir), whereas code-dev-new (lines 61-76, whose own desc is 'Scaffold a new v4 code-dev project') writes `schema-version: v4` + `workflow-step: build` + a `phases/{first-phase}/` tree with 9 stubs. Because the dashboard's v4 surface keys off `schema-version ≡ "v4"` (code-dev.md line 245 `is-v4`), every freshly-created project is v1 and the entire v4 path is dark; e.g. `code-dev resume` immediately bails with 'Legacy v1 project — resume not available' (code-dev-state-resume.md lines 54-58). The v4 scaffolder has no reachable invocation at all.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Run `code-dev new` → answer prompts → project is created by code-dev-init as schema-version v1. Then `code-dev resume` prints 'Legacy v1 project — resume not available'; `code-dev branch`/phase tooling that asserts v4 also fails. There is no command that ever runs code-dev-new.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Delete the line 76 `new → code-dev-init` route (init is the legacy v1 scaffolder) so `new` falls through to line 92 `code-dev-new`; or pick one canonical scaffolder and route both consistently.

confirmed_bugs.title: merge and fold-in silently create cycles — the cycle guard is bypassed by every edge-rewiring op
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: detect_cycle() is only invoked by add_edge() (line 133) and verify() (line 382). merge_nodes() and fold_in() rewire existing edges (e['from']/e['to'] reassigned to the merge/fold target) but never re-run detect_cycle, and their CLI handlers cmd_merge/cmd_fold_in call _save_and_render unconditionally and print {"ok": true} with exit 0. Rewiring can close a cycle: e.g. edges into->x and x->child, fold child into into, yields into->x and x->into. Same for merge: edges design->impl and impl->ship, merge {design,ship}->core yields core->impl and impl->core. critical_path() returns [] on a cyclic graph (line 340) so render_md even succeeds, so the corrupt DAG.json + DAG.md are written with no error. This violates the flagship invariant that DAG.json is always acyclic; the corruption is only ever noticed if a later `dag verify` / dag-consistency run happens to be executed.
confirmed_bugs.repro: bootstrap a project DAG.json with nodes design,impl,test,ship and depends-edges design->impl, design->test, impl->ship, test->ship (an acyclic diamond). Run `dag merge --file DAG.json --ids design,ship --into core --new-label core` (exactly what code-dev-combine.md line 135 issues). It prints {"ok": true} (rc 0) and writes edges core->impl, core->test, impl->core, test->core — a cycle (members test,core,impl). Verified live.
confirmed_bugs.fix: After mutation in merge_nodes/fold_in (and in cmd_merge/cmd_fold_in before saving) call detect_cycle(dag); if it returns non-None, raise/return CYCLE_DETECTED and do NOT write — mirror the add_edge guard.

confirmed_bugs.title: undo of a journaled decision corrupts _decisions.md (op=append but snapshot field is a file PATH, not a byte size)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: code-dev-journal-decision takes a FULL FILE snapshot of _decisions.md (COPY-FILE to {snap-dir}/_decisions.md, line 66) but writes its _actions.log entry with op=`append` and the snapshot field set to that snapshot PATH (line 67-68: `... append {decisions-path} {snap-dir}/_decisions.md`). code-dev-state-undo's `append` branch assumes the snapshot field is a pre-append BYTE SIZE and does `TRUNCATE(target-path, size=snap-path)` (line 79) where snap-path is the string "{proj}/archive/snapshots/act-.../_decisions.md". TRUNCATE is given a path where it expects an integer length, so _decisions.md is truncated to a nonsense/zero length (or the op errors) instead of being restored from the snapshot. The op should have been `replace` (a real file snapshot exists and undo's replace branch would COPY-FILE it back correctly).
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev load proj; code-dev decision (records ADR, writes actions.log entry with op=append + snapshot path); code-dev undo → undo enters the `append` branch and TRUNCATEs _decisions.md to a bad size → all ADRs (and any other content) below the truncation point are destroyed; the actual pre-append content is NOT restored.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In code-dev-journal-decision line 68 change the op from `append` to `replace` (it stores a full snapshot path); OR make undo's append branch detect non-numeric snap-path and route to file-restore.

confirmed_bugs.title: `pr sync` flattens and destroys the entire project `_meta.md` (state corruption + PR rows vanish)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: pr-sync.md calls `TOOL(prefs, set, "--file {proj-dir}/_meta.md --set pr-{pr-num}.ci-status {TOJSON}")`. prefs.set_pref() (prefs.py:24-35) is built only for flat workspace/preferences/*.md files: it (a) computes target = Path(workspace)/"preferences"/file — but file is an ABSOLUTE path so `/` discards the LHS and the real /…/_meta.md is hit, and (b) re-parses the file with parse_pref_file (a flat `key: value` regex that strips indentation, prefs.py:10-20), then REWRITES the whole file as sorted flat lines with a `# runtime-managed preferences (PR-9.7)` header. The nested `pr-3:` block is destroyed: its indented children (slug/phase/state/...) are hoisted to top-level flat keys and the `pr-3:` header line itself is dropped. Verified empirically: after one sync, pr_aggregate.parse_meta(_meta.md) returns [] (was 1 PR block). So every `code-dev pr list` afterward shows zero PRs, and current-pr/next-action/schema-version semantics are scrambled.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Project _meta.md with a `pr-3:` block (e.g. tests/fixtures/compaction/_meta.md). Run `code-dev pr sync 3`. After it, `_meta.md` is a flat sorted key file: the `pr-3:` block is gone and `pr-aggregate`/`pr list` return no PRs. Reproduced directly: `python3 tools/prefs.py --workspace /tmp/x/proj --file /tmp/x/proj/_meta.md --set pr-3.ci-status '{...}'` rewrote the nested file into flat lines.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Do not route _meta.md writes through prefs.set_pref (it is preferences-only). Add a dedicated meta-writer that updates the `pr-N:` block in place (e.g. a `pr_meta set` tool), or store ci-status as a flat `pr-N-ci-status:` top-level key written without rewriting the whole file structure.

confirmed_bugs.title: Write-time hook cannot find the project's _dont-do.md when editing codebase source (walk-up never crosses into my-axon/dev-projects)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The hook claims to be the keystroke guard that makes tokenized dont-dos "ALWAYS enforced across the project" (file header, lines 8-10, 70-73). It finds the governing file by walking UP from the target through `Path(tgt).resolve()` parents (line 59) looking for `_dont-do.md`/`_dont-do-seeds.md`. But the prohibitions live under `<repo>/my-axon/dev-projects/<slug>/phases/<phase>/_dont-do.md`, while the edited source lives in a disjoint codebase repo (e.g. `/work/myrepo/src/x.py`). The project dir is NOT an ancestor of the codebase file, so the walk-up reaches the codebase repo root / filesystem root and returns None (line 64). dont_do_violation then returns None and the write is allowed. So at write-time too, a tokenized prohibition does nothing for real source edits.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Empirically confirmed: created `my-axon/dev-projects/demo/phases/p1/_dont-do.md` with `match: eval(`, then called dont_do_violation('<tmp>/some-repo/src/foo.py', "x = eval('1+1')"). _governing_dont_do returned None and dont_do_violation returned None — eval( write NOT blocked, despite an active tokenized prohibition forbidding it.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Resolve the governing _dont-do from the ACTIVE code-dev project (read W:code-dev-project → {proj-dir}/phases/{phase}/_dont-do.md + seeds) whenever the target is inside that project's codebase, instead of relying on a filesystem-ancestor walk.

confirmed_bugs.title: dag validate flags ALL cycles as defects (ok=false) — over-rejects the flagship's own canonical workflows; contradicts run.md's documented legal back-edges
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _find_cycles() reports every on-complete cycle, and analyze() folds cycles into the ok verdict: `base["ok"] = (... and base["summary"]["cycles"]==0 ...)` (lines 257-259). But workflow-run.md explicitly documents that on-complete back-edges (e.g. self-review->fix s4->s3, audit->plan s6->s2) ARE legal declared targets and the runtime terminates them via the step-count/rejection bound, NOT by being acyclic. So the validator treats the INTENDED control structure as a hard defect. The module docstring itself even admits 'Workflow-run terminates via step-count; the DAG should still be cycle-free for clarity' — i.e. it knowingly downgrades an intentional, runtime-bounded loop to a blocking defect.
confirmed_bugs.repro: python3 tools/workflow_dag.py check --path workspace/domains/code-dev/workflows/code-dev.canonical.yml -> exit 1 (cycles s4->s3, s6->s2). Same for adaptive-free-text.yml (s2->s1) and every other shipped canonical workflow. `analyze` returns ok:false with 'cycle' defects on files that are valid and run correctly.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Demote cycles to informational (like empty_terminals): drop `base["summary"]["cycles"]==0` from the ok computation, or only treat a cycle as a defect when no node on the loop has a guarded/terminal exit (a true unbounded loop).

confirmed_bugs.title: split_node manufactures edges pointing to/from the deleted source node when that node carries a self-dependency, corrupting DAG.json and crashing render with KeyError
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: split_node builds `incoming` (edges with to==node_id) and `outgoing` (edges with from==node_id). A self-edge S->S matches BOTH lists. Lines 268-269 correctly strip every edge touching S, but the re-add loop (270-274) re-introduces S via the OTHER endpoint that was never normalized: for the self-edge as `incoming` it appends {from: e['from']=='S', to: new_id}, and as `outgoing` it appends {from: new_id, to: e['to']=='S'}. Line 275 then deletes node S. Result: edges S->S1, S1->S, S->S2, S2->S all reference the now-deleted S. Unlike its sibling rewirers, split_node has no self-edge/intra-set drop (merge_nodes drops intra-set edges at 244; fold_in strips self-edges at 287). The corrupt graph reaches critical_path (called from render_md:408), where `nodes` excludes S so `indeg` has no 'S' key, and `indeg[e['to']] += 1` at line 329 raises KeyError: 'S'. Verified empirically: split --id S --into-ids S1,S2 on a DAG whose S has a self-edge produces exactly those 4 dangling edges and crashes at dag.py:329. Note the bug is the DANGLING edge, not the self-edge per se: a self-edge with the node still present does NOT crash critical_path (confirmed: returns []).
confirmed_bugs.repro: bootstrap a DAG (level pr); add-node --id S --kind step; inject edge {from:S,to:S,kind:depends} into DAG.json (a self-dep can exist in any hand-authored/migrated DAG.json); then `python3 tools/dag.py split --file DAG.json --id S --into-ids S1,S2`. Observed: Traceback ... critical_path ... line 329, KeyError: 'S', exit 1; on-disk DAG.json now has nodes [S1,S2] and edges [S->S1, S1->S, S->S2, S2->S].
confirmed_bugs.fix: In split_node, when re-adding duplicated edges skip the source's self-references and self-loops: build incoming/outgoing excluding edges where the opposite endpoint is node_id (e.g. `incoming = [e for e in edges if e['to']==node_id and e['from']!=node_id]`, same for outgoing), and after re-adding drop any edge with from==to. Mirror fold_in's `dag['edges'] = [e for e in edges if e['from'] != e['to']]` post-step.

confirmed_bugs.title: `code-dev thaw` runs the FREEZE branch — mode flag never set on the direct thaw route
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: Router routes both verbs to the same program without setting the discriminator: line 118 `freeze → EXEC(code-dev-safety-freeze)` and line 119 `thaw → EXEC(code-dev-safety-freeze)`. Inside code-dev-safety-freeze.md line 44, `mode ← RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-freeze-mode) | "freeze"`. Nothing on the `thaw` route sets W:code-dev-freeze-mode, so it defaults to 'freeze' (or worse, picks up a stale value). The correct caller, code-dev-hold.md, DOES set it (`STORE(W:code-dev-freeze-mode, "thaw")` line 42 before EXEC) — proving the contract. Consequence: `code-dev thaw` re-runs the freeze branch (lines 47-70): it re-snapshots, rewrites workflow-step to 'frozen', appends another 'phase frozen' log + a `phase-frozen` event, and prints 'workflow-step: frozen' instead of thawing. The same defect exists at the second entry point code-dev-safety.md lines 38-39 (`code-dev safety thaw`).
confirmed_bugs.repro: `code-dev freeze "waiting on upstream"` then `code-dev thaw` → output shows 'workflow-step: frozen' and a new 'phase frozen' entry is appended; the phase is never thawed and prior workflow-step is never restored.
confirmed_bugs.fix: On the thaw routes set the mode first: `IF cmd ≡ "thaw" → STORE(W:code-dev-freeze-mode, "thaw") → EXEC(code-dev-safety-freeze)` (and likewise in code-dev-safety.md). Or route freeze/thaw through code-dev-hold, which already sets the flag.

confirmed_bugs.title: `code-dev done` / `code-dev skip` reference an unbound `meta` variable for the phase fallback
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: LOAD CONTEXT (lines 45-46) binds only `cmd` and `project` — not `meta`. `meta` is first assigned at line 212 (inside `IF cmd ≡ "load"`) and line 220 (DASHBOARD DATA), both of which are BELOW and after the done/back/skip routes, which EXEC/DONE at lines 127-177. The SHADOW GATE binds `meta-gate` (a different name) and only when `cmd ∈ {study,plan,pr,log,audit,explain}` — done/skip are not in that set. Yet line 129 `done-phase ← RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-done-phase) | meta.phase` and line 152 `skip-phase ← RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-skip-phase) | meta.phase` dereference `meta.phase`. Since W:code-dev-done-phase and W:code-dev-skip-phase are never STORE'd anywhere (grep confirms), the `meta.phase` fallback is the PRIMARY path for the bare commands, so it fires immediately: null/undefined phase gets passed to `TOOL(phase-model, done, --phase {done-phase})` (line 130) / skip-guard (line 162), producing a wrong-target or errored call. The adjacent `back` route (line 140) deliberately falls back to a QUERY instead of `meta.phase` — confirming the author knew `meta` is unavailable here and that done/skip are the oversight.
confirmed_bugs.repro: On a loaded project run bare `code-dev done` (no phase arg) → W:code-dev-done-phase is ∅ → evaluates `meta.phase` but `meta` was never read → phase-model.done is invoked with an empty/garbage `--phase`, marking the wrong phase done or erroring. Same with `code-dev skip`.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Read meta before the fallback: `done-phase ← RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-done-phase) | READ("{W:myaxon-dev-projects}/{project}/_meta.md").phase` (and identically for skip-phase), or hoist a single `meta ← READ(...)` into LOAD CONTEXT.

confirmed_bugs.title: `code-dev back` commits stale-downstream then ignores a failing `advance`, leaving a half-mutated + misreported phase manifest
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The `back` route runs `stale_downstream(back-phase)` (line 141, writes to _phases.json immediately) and then `advance(back-phase)` (line 142) WITHOUT binding or checking advance's result. `advance` raises ValueError / exits 1 whenever back-phase's own deps are not `done` (phase_model.py:97-100). But lines 143-147 unconditionally print `Re-entered PHASE {back-phase}` and `Downstream is now STALE: {staled.staled}`. So advance fails silently: the re-entered phase stays in its old status (e.g. `stale`), is NOT made `active`, yet the user is told it was re-entered AND its descendants were just force-staled on disk. The `done` route right above (line 131) DOES check `result.ok` — the `back` route is the unguarded one. Compounding it, stale_downstream and advance are two separate load/save ops, so the descendant-stale write on line 141 is already committed before advance is even attempted.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Default ladder, all phases done. Run `code-dev back study` (study->active, plan/pr/log/audit->stale). Then run `code-dev back pr`: stale_downstream(pr) re-stales log+audit and saves; advance(pr) raises `cannot advance to 'pr': deps not done: ['plan']` (plan is stale); program prints the success banner anyway. Verified: final state pr=stale (NOT active), log/audit re-staled, banner claims re-entry succeeded.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In the back route, do `advance` first and bind `res ← TOOL(phase-model, advance, ...)`; only call stale-downstream and print the banner when `res.ok ≡ true`, else FAIL with res.error (mirror the done route).

confirmed_bugs.title: `_is_stub` flags any header-only artifact as an empty stub, so the phase-gate false-positives on real header-outline plans/PR lists (hard block in enforce mode)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: `_is_stub` builds `body` by dropping every line where `ln.lstrip().startswith('#')`, i.e. ALL markdown headers at any depth. A populated `02-plan.md` whose substance is a section outline (`## Goals`/`## Approach`/`## Risks`) or a `02-prs.md` whose PRs are header lines (`## PR-1: <title text>`) has zero surviving non-header lines, so `body == ''` and the artifact is reported `stub-only`. The DEFAULT_CONTRACT marker check (`marker in text`) does not save it because the scaffold marker `_Run: code-dev plan_` is absent in the populated file. In warn mode this is a spurious ⚠; with L:phase-gate-enforce=true the gate FAILs closed (code-dev-phase-new.md:57-58), hard-blocking advancement past a genuinely complete phase.
confirmed_bugs.repro:
phases/2-foundation/02-plan.md = `# Plan

## Goals
## Approach
## Risks
`; 02-prs.md = `# PR list

## PR-1: Real substantive content.
`. `phase-gate check --project … --phase 2-foundation` returns missing=[plan stub-only, prs stub-only], satisfied=false. Adding a single non-`#` body line flips it to satisfied — proving headers are treated as non-content.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Only strip a single leading H1/title line (or count header lines as content); e.g. keep lines that are headers carrying text beyond the marker, or treat `body` as empty only when there are zero non-blank lines at all after dropping just the first `# ` line.

confirmed_bugs.title: Ledger note containing a carriage return (or U+2028/U+2029/\f) silently drops the recorded phase row from the G-02 ledger
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: `_sanitize` only replaces `\n` and `|`; it does NOT strip `\r` or other Unicode line separators. `append_row` writes the row as one physical line, but `read_rows` parses via `Path.read_text().splitlines()`, which ALSO splits on `\r`, `\r\n`, `\v`, `\f`, `\x1c-\x1e`, ` `, ` `. A note with an embedded `\r` is written as `| ts | prog | phase | line1\rline2 |`; on read it becomes two lines, `| ts | prog | phase | line1` (fails the 4-column `_ROW_RE`, dropped) and `line2 |` (no leading `|`, skipped). The phase transition vanishes from list/status/verify. Since notes routinely carry tool/command output, a CR is easy to hit, and the loss is silent — directly undermining the G-02 'append-only, every transition recorded' guarantee.
confirmed_bugs.repro: append_row(ws,'P','EXECUTE','line1\rline2'); append_row(ws,'P','DONE','ok'); read_rows(ws) returns only the DONE row — the EXECUTE phase row is gone. Verified count=1.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In `_sanitize`, normalize all line breaks: `re.sub(r'[\r\n  \x0b\x0c\x1c-\x1e]+', ' ', s)` (or `.replace('\r',' ')` at minimum) before the `|` replacement.

confirmed_bugs.title: critical_path() raises KeyError on a dangling edge, so render and every mutation crash on a DAG that verify is meant to flag
confirmed_bugs.line: 325-329 (critical_path); reached via render_md line 408 and _save_and_render line 594
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: critical_path builds adjacency directly from edges with `adj[e['from']].append(e['to'])` and `indeg[e['to']] += 1` (lines 327-329) but indeg is initialized only for ids in nodes (line 324). detect_cycle() guards this with `if e['from'] in nodes and e['to'] in nodes` (line 300); critical_path has NO such guard. So if any edge references a node id that is not in nodes (a dangling edge — exactly the DANGLING_EDGE_FROM/TO condition verify is designed to report), `indeg[e['to']] += 1` throws KeyError. Because render_md() calls critical_path() and _save_and_render() calls render_md() after every node/edge op, any DAG.json that contains a dangling edge cannot be rendered, set-status'd, add-node'd, etc. — each dies with an unhandled KeyError traceback instead of producing output. The consistency tool can detect the dangling edge but dag.py cannot even render the file to help fix it.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Create DAG.json with nodes a,b and edges [{from:a,to:b},{from:b,to:ghost}] (ghost not a node — e.g. a hand-edited file or an edge left after an out-of-band node deletion). Run any of `dag render --file DAG.json`, `dag set-status --file DAG.json --id a --status active`, `dag add-node ...` — all crash with `KeyError: 'ghost'`. Verified live.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In critical_path add the same endpoint guard detect_cycle uses: `if e.get('kind','depends')!='depends' or e['from'] not in indeg or e['to'] not in indeg: continue` before touching adj/indeg.

confirmed_bugs.title: cmd_add_edge cycle handling is dead code — a cycle-closing edge crashes the CLI with a traceback instead of the documented return 3 / {ok:false}
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: cmd_add_edge calls add_edge(...) on line 608, THEN on lines 609-613 checks `cyc = detect_cycle(dag)` and tries to return code 3 with {"ok": false, "code": "CYCLE_DETECTED"}. But add_edge() already detects the cycle internally (line 133), pops the edge, and RAISES ValueError (line 135). The exception propagates out of cmd_add_edge before line 609 is ever reached, so the graceful branch is unreachable dead code. main() does not catch it, so the process exits 1 with a Python traceback on stderr rather than the structured JSON + exit 3 the handler was written to emit.
confirmed_bugs.repro: DAG.json with nodes a,b and edge a->b. Run `python3 dag.py add-edge --file DAG.json --from b --to a`. Observed: exit code 1, stderr ends with `ValueError: adding edge b->a would create a cycle (CYCLE_DETECTED)` and a full traceback — not `{"ok": false, "code": "CYCLE_DETECTED"}` / rc 3. Verified live.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Wrap the add_edge call in try/except ValueError in cmd_add_edge and emit the JSON error + return 3 there (and delete the now-redundant post-call detect_cycle block), or have add_edge return a status instead of raising.

confirmed_bugs.title: undo of a log entry is a silent no-op — pre-append size is captured AFTER the append
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: code-dev-journal-log APPENDs the new entry to 04-log.md FIRST (line 204), and only THEN computes `pre-size ← FILE-SIZE(log-file)` (line 207) and writes it as the append-snapshot in _actions.log (line 209). Despite the comment 'record pre-append size', the size captured is the POST-append size. code-dev-state-undo's append branch does `TRUNCATE(target-path, size=snap-path)` (line 79) using that size, which equals the current file length → truncation is a no-op. The log entry is never removed, so `code-dev undo` of a log write does nothing.
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev load proj; code-dev log (appends an entry, records post-append size as the 'pre-size'); code-dev undo → confirms, runs TRUNCATE to the post-append size → 04-log.md is unchanged, the entry that was supposedly undone is still present.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Capture pre-size BEFORE the append: move `pre-size ← IF FILE-EXISTS(log-file) → FILE-SIZE(log-file) | 0` and the _actions.log APPEND above line 204 (compute size, then append entry).

confirmed_bugs.title: freeze → undo leaves project _meta.md stuck 'frozen' (only the phase _meta.md mutation is recorded in _actions.log)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: safety-freeze mutates BOTH files — phase _meta.md (line 58-59) and project _meta.md (line 60-61) — and snapshots BOTH (phase_meta.md line 54, proj_meta.md line 55). But it appends only ONE _actions.log entry, for the phase file (line 56-57: `... replace {phase-dir}/_meta.md {snap-dir}/phase_meta.md`). The proj_meta.md snapshot is never referenced by any log line. code-dev undo of a freeze therefore restores only the phase _meta.md; the project _meta.md keeps `workflow-step: frozen`. Result: phase says (e.g.) 'build' while project says 'frozen' — a divergent, half-reverted state that every reader (status, resume, next) then disagrees on.
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev load proj; code-dev freeze 'blocked' (both metas → frozen, one actions.log entry); code-dev undo → phase _meta.md reverts to prior workflow-step, project _meta.md still reads `workflow-step: frozen`. `code-dev status` now shows project workflow-step=frozen but phase=build.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Append a SECOND _actions.log entry for the project meta (`{action-id}b replace {proj-dir}/_meta.md {snap-dir}/proj_meta.md`), or make undo restore every snapshot recorded under one action-id.

confirmed_bugs.title: resume always drops reviewer state — reads reviewer-state.md from a wrong, non-existent nested path
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: resume Step 9 reads `reviewer-path ← "{phase-dir}/03-prs/{current-pr}/reviewer-state.md"` (line 115). Every other program in the layer (status:53, handoff:54, metrics:47, next:92, pr-respond:43/72, preflight, explain-reviewer, since, new, phase-new) stores/reads reviewer-state at `{phase-dir}/reviewer-state.md` — there is NO `03-prs/{pr}/` subdirectory for it anywhere. So resume's READ always misses, reviewer-state stays ∅, reviewer-open=0, and the briefing renders 'No PR in review' / '0 open objection(s)' (lines 182-186) even when open objections exist. For a compaction-recovery tool whose entire purpose is to not lose context, it silently loses reviewer state on every run.
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev load proj where {phase}/reviewer-state.md has open objections; code-dev resume → 'REVIEWER STATE: No PR in review' and the marker line writes 'reviewer: no PR in review', despite open objections being present and visible via `code-dev status`.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Change line 115 to `reviewer-path ← "{phase-dir}/reviewer-state.md"` (drop the `/03-prs/{current-pr}/` segment), matching every other program.

confirmed_bugs.title: `pr_sync` discards all CI results exactly when checks are pending or failing
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: `gh pr checks` exits non-zero precisely when checks are not all green — exit code 8 when checks are still pending (documented), exit 1 when any check fails. pr_sync.py:20-21 does `if r.returncode != 0: return {"ok": False, "reason": ...}` and never parses r.stdout, even though gh has already emitted valid JSON for the pending/failing checks. So the pass/pending/fail tallying loop (26-34) only ever executes on the fully-green path; in the two states a sync is most needed (pending / failing CI) it returns ok:False with no counts. pr-sync.md then prints/EMITs `{result-json.pass} pending {result-json.pending} {result-json.fail}` which are absent.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Open a PR whose CI is still running, or has a failing check. `gh pr checks <pr> --json name,status,conclusion` prints JSON but exits 8 (pending) or 1 (fail). `pr_sync.sync(pr)` returns `{ok:False, reason:'gh non-zero'}` — the actual check breakdown is thrown away; sync reports nothing useful.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Parse `r.stdout` first; only treat returncode as fatal when stdout is empty/unparseable. Tally from rows regardless of returncode (gh exit 8/1 means pending/fail, not tool error).

confirmed_bugs.title: drift / export / pr-ready read the wrong spec filename (case + zero-pad mismatch); drift also crashes on the miss
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: code-dev-pr-create writes specs as `03-prs/PR-{pad(pr-n,3)}.md` (uppercase, zero-padded — PR-003.md; confirmed by fixtures PR-00N.md). But drift.md:37 and export.md:37 build `03-prs/pr-{pr-num}.md` (lowercase, no pad) → `pr-3.md`, which never exists. pr-ready.md:93 builds `03-prs/pr-{W:code-dev-pr-create}.md` where the var is already `PR-007`, yielding `pr-PR-007.md`. (github.md:42-43 correctly uses `{pr}.md`, proving create's convention.) Worse, pr_drift.drift() (pr_drift.py:30) calls `pr_spec.read_text()` with no existence guard, so the casing miss raises FileNotFoundError and crashes the tool instead of returning a result. Verified: pr_drift.drift(Path('/no/such/pr-3.md'), …) → FileNotFoundError.
confirmed_bugs.repro: `code-dev pr 3` writes PR-003.md. Then `code-dev pr drift 3` → tool opens `03-prs/pr-3.md` → FileNotFoundError (drift never runs). `code-dev pr-ready PR-007 --strict` evaluates rules against `pr-PR-007.md` (nonexistent).
confirmed_bugs.fix: Use the create convention everywhere: spec path = `03-prs/PR-{zero-pad-3(pr-num)}.md`; in drift.md/export.md uppercase+pad the arg, in pr-ready.md drop the extra `pr-` prefix. Add an existence guard in pr_drift.drift() returning `{ok:False, reason:'spec not found'}`.

confirmed_bugs.title: drift detector has a near-100% false-negative rate (drift goes undetected)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: For each acceptance item, drift tokenizes to words matching `[a-z][\w_\-]{3,}` (any word ≥4 chars) and marks the item MET if ANY ONE token appears anywhere in the lowercased `git diff HEAD` text — which includes added lines, REMOVED lines, surrounding context, and the diff/file headers (line 47: `if not any(tok in diff for tok in toks)`). Common English words (input, update, error, handle, value, README, examples…) almost always appear somewhere in a real diff, so unrelated/unimplemented items are reported as satisfied. Verified: item 'The new function process_batch handles empty input' is marked MET against a diff that never defines process_batch, solely because the word 'input' appears in an unrelated comment. Because removed-line and context text count too, 'remove deprecated_helper' can read as MET even if the symbol was re-added.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Spec acceptance: `- [ ] process_batch handles empty input`. Implement nothing related, but have any diff hunk containing the word 'input' (very common). `code-dev pr drift N` → '✓ no spec-vs-diff drift detected'. Real drift missed.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Require a stronger signal: match only on added (`^+`) lines, ignore stop-words and tokens <5 chars, and require a majority/anchored token (function/identifier with `_` or CamelCase) rather than any single common word; or flag items as 'weak-match' rather than MET.

confirmed_bugs.title: Coverage gate false-PASSES on every committed PR (diffs working-tree-vs-HEAD, not branch-vs-base)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The changed-file set is computed as `diff ← TOOL(shell, "git -C {codebase} diff --name-only HEAD")` (line 39). Two-dot `git diff HEAD` reports only UNCOMMITTED changes (working tree + index vs the current commit). Every other review/safety program in AXON correctly uses `{base}...HEAD` (review-diff L46/48, review-scope L43, review-self L55). In AXON's actual workflow a PR's work is committed on a branch and pushed; at the point coverage would run, `git diff HEAD` returns EMPTY, so `changed` is `[]`, the `FOR file ∈ changed` loop body never executes, `uncovered` stays `[]`, and the program prints `✓ all changed lines covered` (L49) having inspected zero lines. The coverage gate thus reports OK on a fully-committed, totally-uncovered PR.
confirmed_bugs.repro: On a feature branch, implement a PR and `git commit` it (no uncommitted changes). Run `code-dev review coverage cov.json`. `git diff --name-only HEAD` is empty → output: `✓ all changed lines covered`, regardless of cov.json contents.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Compute base like the sibling programs and diff `{base}...HEAD`: `diff ← TOOL(shell, "git -C {codebase} diff --name-only {base}...HEAD")` where `base ← (READ _profile.md).base-branch | "master"`.

confirmed_bugs.title: Coverage gate silently passes files with ZERO coverage (file absent from coverage JSON is skipped)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The loop guards line-level inspection with `IF data.{file} ≠ ∅` (L43): a changed file is only checked when it has an entry in the coverage JSON. A file that was changed but is entirely uninstrumented / never executed by the test run is typically ABSENT from coverage tools' JSON output (not present with all-zero hits). Such a file fails the `data.{file} ≠ ∅` guard, contributes nothing to `uncovered`, and the gate concludes `✓ all changed lines covered` (L49). So the one case the gate exists to catch — a changed file with no coverage at all — is exactly the case it ignores. This is a coverage check that passes on no coverage.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Change `src/foo.cpp` (new file, no test touches it). Coverage JSON has keys only for previously-tested files and omits `src/foo.cpp` entirely. Run the gate → `data."src/foo.cpp" ≡ ∅` → skipped → `✓ all changed lines covered`.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Treat a changed (non-test) file missing from `data` as fully uncovered: `ELSE: APPEND(uncovered, "{file}: (no coverage data — 0% instrumented)")` in an else branch of the `IF data.{file} ≠ ∅` check.

confirmed_bugs.title: Self-review gate rubber-stamps: acceptance criteria matched against file NAMES, not diff content
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: `changed-files ← TOOL(shell, "git ... diff --name-only {base}...HEAD")` (L55) is the list of changed PATHS only. The gap loop (L59-64) extracts each criterion's backtick token(s) and counts hits via `IF changed-files CONTAINS k` — i.e. it asks whether the keyword appears anywhere in the concatenated FILE PATHS, never in the actual code change. A criterion is dropped from `gaps` as soon as one of its backtick tokens is a substring of any changed path. So a criterion like ``- [ ] `Summary` writes SMSPEC header`` is marked satisfied merely because a file `summary.cpp` was touched, even if the diff does the opposite. Preflight Gate 4 then reads `selfrev.gaps ≡ 0 → pass` (preflight L93). The diff sibling program correctly greps `diff-content` (review-diff L82); self-review does not. Result: the self-review gate green-lights work that only touched the right filenames. Independently, the diff-stat captured (L54, `--stat`) is also names+counts only, never content, so nothing in this program ever inspects the actual change.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Spec criterion ``- [ ] `keywordLB` resolved at parse time``. Make a no-op edit to a file whose path contains `keyword` (or whose name a token matches). Run self-review/preflight: hits>0 → criterion not a gap → `Gaps identified: 0` → Gate 4 PASS, despite the behavior being absent.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Match against diff content, not names: add `diff-content ← TOOL(shell, "git ... diff {base}...HEAD")` and test `IF diff-content CONTAINS k`; keep filename match only as a weak secondary signal.

confirmed_bugs.title: Preflight Gate 6 false-PASSES open reviewer objections when no PR is resolved (pr ≡ ∅)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: `pr` is allowed to be empty: `pr ← RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-pr-create) | meta.current-pr | ∅` (L44). Gate 6 builds its detector by interpolating `pr` into a regex: `my-pr-open ← COUNT(EXTRACT(state, pattern="\\| {pr} \\|.*\\| open \\|", mode=lines))` (L107). When `pr ≡ ∅`, the interpolated pattern is `\| ∅ \|.*\| open \|` (or `\|  \|.*\| open \|`), which cannot match a real reviewer-state row such as `| PR-007 | bska | R3 | … | open |` (col0 is a concrete PR id, per pr-respond L73). So `my-pr-open` is 0 and Gate 6 emits `pass: no open objections on ` (L108) even though unresolved blocking objections exist for the actual PR. A safety gate whose entire job is to block on open objections reports clean because the PR id wasn't resolved.
confirmed_bugs.repro: reviewer-state.md has `| PR-007 | bska | R3 | bug X | open |`. Run `code-dev preflight` in a context where W:code-dev-pr-create is unset and meta.current-pr is absent → pr=∅ → Gate 6 pattern matches nothing → `✓ Gate 6 — reviewer-pr  no open objections on`. Gate 0..4 / overall may show READY.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Fail-closed when pr is unknown: `IF pr ≡ ∅ → results += [{id:6,status:"fail",msg:"PR unresolved — cannot verify objections"}]` before running the regex; otherwise keep the per-PR match.

confirmed_bugs.title: promote drops the match: token — converts an enforceable prohibition into a prose-only one in seeds (and cascaded phases)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: A tokenized prohibition is stored as two lines: `- <text>` then an indented `match: <tok>` (line 70). `promote` appends to seeds only `"- {arg}    _(promoted ...)_\n"` (line 99) — it copies the bullet text `{arg}` but NOT the indented `match:` line, because the match token lives on a separate line and `arg` is just the prohibition text. `--cascade` does the same to every other active phase's `_dont-do.md` (lines 108-109). Result: the promoted/cascaded copy is classified `prose` (no match:, no review:) by the shared parser. That is exactly the fail-closed BLOCK case for R_DONT_DO (r_dont_do.py:125-128), R_DONT_DO_LINT, and Gate 3. So promoting a correctly-captured rule manufactures an un-enforceable rule, and the write-time hook (which does read _dont-do-seeds.md, hook line 60) skips it because it has no token.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Empirically confirmed: tokenized phase bullet `- no eval / match: eval(` lints clean; after promote the seeds entry is `- no eval _(promoted...)_` with no match line → lint_text flags it as prose-only (un-enforceable).
confirmed_bugs.fix: On promote/cascade, carry the source bullet's `match:`/`review:` line too: APPEND `"- {arg} _(promoted...)_\n  match: {match-tok}\n"` (look the token up from the source bullet).

confirmed_bugs.title: Seeding a new phase from prose-only seeds makes Gate 3 fail-closed BLOCK on a freshly-started phase
confirmed_bugs.line: code-dev-phase-start.md:48-52 (copies seed-content verbatim into {phase-dir}/_dont-do.md); code-dev-safety-preflight.md:78-89 (Gate 3 fail-closed on any prose)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: phase-start copies `seed-content` (raw seeds bullets) verbatim into the new phase's `_dont-do.md` (lines 50-52). Because promote wrote prose-only bullets into seeds (Bug 3), every newly started phase inherits prose-only prohibitions. Gate 3 lints `{phase-dir}/_dont-do.md` via `dont-do-lint lint` and FAILS fail-closed on any prose-only entry (code-dev-safety-preflight.md:87-89, prose>0 → status fail). So immediately after `code-dev phase start`, preflight is BLOCKED with no code change at fault, and the only escape is to manually re-tokenize each seeded line. Even seeds added by hand per the code-dev-new template guidance ("e.g. 'never commit generated files'", code-dev-new.md:90) are prose and will block.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Empirically confirmed: seeds containing `- no eval _(promoted...)_` copied into a new phase _dont-do.md → lint_text reports it as prose → Gate 3 status=fail (BLOCK) on the new phase with zero code edits.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Either preserve match:/review: lines through promote→seed (Bug 3) so seeded entries stay enforceable, or have Gate 3 treat newly-seeded-but-untokenized entries as a WARN until first edit instead of a hard BLOCK.

confirmed_bugs.title: advance-guard is structurally a no-op in fixed mode — it can never detect a skipped or out-of-order node
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The guard is advertised as 'the real teeth' that block node-jumps, but it only validates single-step adjacency against the SAME data the caller used to choose the step. In workflow-run.md the loop resolves next-id directly from cursor.on-complete[].next (lines 143-146), then calls advance(cursor.id, next-id) (line 151). advance() recomputes the allowed set via next_allowed(), which is literally the list of cursor.on-complete[].next values (workflow_run.py:36-44). So requested_next is, by construction, always a member of `allowed` for a fixed-mode run — the `if requested_next in allowed: return` branch (line 56) always succeeds. The guard cannot reject anything the program would actually do. Detecting a genuine SKIP (e.g. jumping s1->s4 in code-dev.canonical.yml, bypassing plan/pr-create/self-review) would require comparing the transition against recorded trajectory/declared start, which advance() never does. It validates one edge of the graph using the graph itself, so any traversal that follows declared edges — including one that omitted earlier mandatory nodes because the cursor was set wrong (see start-node bug) — passes. There is no monotonicity or visited-set check anywhere.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Run any fixed workflow (e.g. code-dev.canonical.yml). At every transition the guard returns ok:true because next-id was read from cursor.on-complete moments earlier. Now corrupt the cursor so the loop starts at s4 (self-review) — every subsequent guard call (s4->s5->s6->s7) still returns ok:true because s5/s6/s7 are declared targets of their predecessors; the skipped s1/s2/s3 are never noticed. The guard provides zero protection against a skip.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Make advance() validate against run state, not just the static graph: pass the prior trajectory (or expected predecessor) and reject a transition whose `from` is not the actual last-recorded node, and verify the cursor at loop entry equals declared start; the single-edge check alone cannot enforce in-order traversal.

confirmed_bugs.title: R_WORKFLOW_NODE_ORDER status check is case-sensitive — capitalized statuses (Done/DONE/Active) bypass the gate entirely, even in BLOCK mode
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _violations_in_manifest reads st = p.get('status') and tests `if st in ('active','done')` with raw, case-sensitive values, and the dependency check at line 59 compares `byid.get(d,{}).get('status') != 'done'` (also lowercase-literal). A phase whose status is 'Done', 'DONE', 'Active', or 'ACTIVE' is NOT recognized as active/done, so the out-of-order check is skipped for it — the violation is never emitted. The rule's own _norm() helper (line 27-30) uppercases status for the changed-file list, showing the module is aware status casing varies, yet the core check never normalizes. This rule is the merge-gate complement specifically meant to 'catch out-of-band edits to _phases.json that jump nodes'; a hand-edited manifest is exactly where non-canonical casing appears. The longterm flag workspace/memory/longterm/workflow-node-order-required.md currently reads `true`, so the rule runs at BLOCK severity — meaning this casing trick defeats a BLOCKING gate.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Hand-edit a project's _phases.json to {phases:[{id:a,status:pending},{id:b,status:Done,deps:[a]}]} (b done before dep a, but 'Done' capitalized) and stage it. rule.check returns None (verified: _violations_in_manifest returns []), so the out-of-order jump passes the BLOCK gate silently. Lowercase 'done' would have been blocked.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Normalize before comparison: st = str(p.get('status','')).lower() and compare deps with str(byid.get(d,{}).get('status','')).lower() != 'done'.

confirmed_bugs.title: simulate's predicted path diverges from run on any cyclic workflow — simulate breaks on first revisit, run loops to the step-count bound; predicted step count and path are a lie
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: simulate's TRACE has `IF next-id IN visited -> print 'revisit' ; BREAK` (lines 72-74), terminating the walk the instant any node would be re-entered. workflow-run.md has NO such guard: its loop (lines 84-154) re-enters visited nodes freely and only terminates when next-id becomes empty (line 147) or the rejection-criterion fires mid-loop (lines 128-133), which for adaptive-free-text is `state.steps > 25` -> ~26 iterations. So for any workflow with a back-edge, simulate reports a truncated path/step-count while run executes the loop many more times. Simulate is supposed to 'emit the path the run would take' (file header) — it does not.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Workflow a->b->a (unconditional), execution-mode adaptive, rejection-criterion 'state.steps > 25'. Verified: predicate `state.steps > 25` is false at 25 and true at 26, so run does ~26 steps; simulate prints step1 a, step2 b, then 'revisit a' and BREAKs at visited=2. Predicted visited=2 vs actual ~26.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Make simulate mirror run's termination exactly: drop the revisit-BREAK and instead evaluate the rejection-criterion each iteration (it already builds ctx with state.steps at line 62), bounding the walk identically to run; keep max-steps only as a backstop.

confirmed_bugs.title: `start:` is ignored by run and simulate (both use synapses[0]); validate roots reachability/cycle analysis at `start:` — a schema-valid start != synapses[0] makes validate analyze a graph nobody executes
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: analyze() resolves the entry as top-level `start:` (line 175) and computes reachability/orphans/cycles from it (`_reachable_from(start_id, ...)` line 223). But workflow-run.md (line 79) and workflow-simulate.md (line 50) both set `cursor <- wf.synapses[0]`, ignoring `start:` entirely. The schema REQUIRES `start`. When start != synapses[0] (legal and schema-valid), validate's orphan/reachability/cycle verdicts describe a different rooted graph than the one run/simulate traverse: validate can call synapses[0]'s subtree 'orphan' while run actually starts there, and can certify a node reachable that run can never reach.
confirmed_bugs.repro: entry2.yml: start=hub, synapses=[lonely_first(terminal), hub->worker, worker]. validate: reachable=[hub,worker], flags lonely_first as ORPHAN. But run/simulate start at synapses[0]=lonely_first (a terminal) and stop after 1 step, never touching hub/worker. validate's analysis is rooted at the wrong node. (All shipped files currently have start==synapses[0], masking the bug.)
confirmed_bugs.fix: Pick ONE entry contract: change run.md/simulate.md to start at the synapse whose id == wf.start (falling back to synapses[0]), so validate, simulate and run all root at the same node.

confirmed_bugs.title: cmd_split persists the mutated graph to disk BEFORE rendering and has no post-mutation cycle/dangling guard, turning the render crash into permanent, irrecoverable DAG.json corruption
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _save_and_render writes the file via _atomic_write (line 592) and ONLY THEN calls render_md (594). So when render_md->critical_path crashes (bug #1), the corrupt graph has already been os.replace'd onto DAG.json. cmd_split also omits the safety check that cmd_add_edge performs: cmd_add_edge runs detect_cycle after the mutation and returns code 3 with {ok:false,CYCLE_DETECTED} WITHOUT saving if a cycle exists (609-613), whereas cmd_split (and cmd_merge/cmd_fold_in) call _save_and_render unconditionally. Because every mutating command routes through _save_and_render->critical_path, the persisted dangling edges mean every subsequent dag command on that file (even a benign set-status) now also crashes with the same KeyError - the DAG is bricked. The divide program (workspace/programs/code-dev-divide.md:86 phase, :128 pr) treats this DAG.json as the single source of truth, so `code-dev divide` on any phase/PR node with a self-dep permanently corrupts it.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Same setup as bug #1. After the `split` traceback, run `python3 tools/dag.py set-status --file DAG.json --id S1 --status active` -> also crashes KeyError:'S' at critical_path (or read the file: it persists nodes [S1,S2] with 4 dangling edges). The split's stdout never emits the documented {ok:...} JSON.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In _save_and_render, render BEFORE the atomic write (or render to a temp string first) so a render failure aborts the commit; and/or add a detect_cycle/dangling-edge guard in cmd_split (and cmd_merge/cmd_fold_in) mirroring cmd_add_edge: after the mutation, if detect_cycle(dag) or any dangling edge, print {ok:false,...} to stderr and return non-zero without saving. Fixing bug #1 removes the trigger, but the write-then-render ordering is independently unsafe for any future render error.

confirmed_bugs.title: `code-dev phase list` dead-routes to a non-existent program (the prescribed recovery action in 5 places)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: Line 96 is the sole `phase` route: `IF cmd ≡ "phase" → EXEC(code-dev-phase-{W:code-dev-phase-cmd | 'new'})`. It does literal program-name interpolation: the subcommand token is concatenated into the program name. Only two phase programs exist on disk — code-dev-phase-new.md and code-dev-phase-start.md (verified `ls code-dev-phase-*`); there is NO code-dev-phase-list.md anywhere in the tree. There is no `list` branch, alias, or fallthrough. So `code-dev phase list` interpolates to EXEC(code-dev-phase-list) → an unresolved program / dead route. This is not a corner case: five error/next-suggestion sites prescribe exactly this command as the recovery action for a missing-phase error — code-dev-combine.md:58 (fix + suggested_next), code-dev-divide.md:45 (fix + suggested_next), code-dev-phase-new.md:40 and :42 (suggested_next), code-dev-phase-start.md:40 (suggested_next). The 'available phases' listing those errors promise exists in no program. So the documented fix for the partition/divide/combine missing-phase failure routes the user straight into another dead end, and there is no way to actually list phases.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Load a v4 project, run `code-dev combine` (or `divide`) referencing a phase that isn't on disk → FAIL fires with fix="Run: code-dev phase list to see available phases". Follow that instruction: `code-dev phase list` → line 96 resolves EXEC(code-dev-phase-list) → no such program → unresolved/dead route, no phase listing ever shown.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Add a real `list` handler before/at the phase route, e.g. `IF cmd ≡ "phase" AND W:code-dev-phase-cmd ≡ "list" → EXEC(code-dev-phase-list)` and create code-dev-phase-list.md (enumerate phases/* dirs from phase-model/DAG). Minimal alternative: create code-dev-phase-list.md and ensure the route can reach it; or repoint all 5 suggested_next/fix strings to `code-dev status` (which already renders phases) until a lister exists.

confirmed_bugs.title: _save_and_render persists the mutated DAG (atomic os.replace) BEFORE render_md runs, so a render crash leaves a durably-corrupt DAG.json on disk
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _save_and_render does `_atomic_write(path, dag)` at line 592 FIRST (an os.replace at line 60 — durable, the tmp file is renamed over the canonical DAG.json), and only THEN calls `render_md(path)` at line 594. render_md re-reads the just-written file and calls critical_path (line 408), which KeyErrors on any dangling edge (see bug #2). Because the write already committed, the crash happens AFTER the corrupt graph is on disk. Every mutator routes through this helper: cmd_merge (655), cmd_split (665), cmd_fold_in (673), cmd_build_from_prs (622), cmd_remove_node (630), cmd_add_node (601). So any of these that produces a dangling edge (or that runs against a DAG with a pre-existing dangling edge) exits non-zero yet has already overwritten DAG.json with the mutated, corrupt graph (and bumped the `generated` timestamp at line 591). The operation looks failed to the caller but silently mutated state. Verified end-to-end: `merge --ids A,B --into C` on a DAG containing edge A->GHOST crashes KeyError:'GHOST' at line 329, yet on-disk DAG.json afterward is nodes=['C'], edges=[('C','GHOST')] with a fresh timestamp.
confirmed_bugs.repro: echo DAG.json with nodes A,B and edges [A->GHOST(depends), A->B] (GHOST not a node). Run `python3 tools/dag.py merge --file DAG.json --ids A,B --into C`. Process exits 1 with KeyError:'GHOST' from line 329, BUT `cat DAG.json` shows the fully-merged graph (nodes ['C'], edges [('C','GHOST')]) already written. The mutation was not rolled back.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Render to a temp/in-memory string and validate BEFORE committing the JSON: in _save_and_render, build the md text from the in-memory `dag` (not by re-reading), and only call _atomic_write for DAG.json after render succeeds — i.e. swap the order and make render operate on the in-memory dag so both succeed-or-neither-commits. Alternatively wrap the write+render in try/except that restores the previous file contents on failure (read old bytes before _atomic_write, rewrite them in the except).

confirmed_bugs.title: critical_path crashes with KeyError on any dangling edge because it omits the node-membership guard that detect_cycle has
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: critical_path builds `indeg` only for ids in `nodes` (line 324), but its edge loop at 325-329 does `adj[e['from']].append(e['to'])` and `indeg[e['to']] += 1` with NO check that from/to are actually nodes. If an edge points to a non-node id (dangling edge, e.g. to='GHOST'), `indeg['GHOST'] += 1` raises KeyError because 'GHOST' was never inserted into the dict. (The topo loop at 336 `for v in adj[u]` would also visit ghost ids, but the indeg increment crashes first.) detect_cycle (line 300) and descendants (line 205) both guard with `if e['from'] in nodes and e['to'] in nodes`; critical_path is the lone graph walk that forgot it. This is the actual exception behind every render/merge/split/fold-in crash on a dangling DAG, and it is what bricks `dag render` against a corrupt file. Verified: critical_path({nodes:[A,B], edges:[A->GHOST, A->B]}) raises KeyError:'GHOST'; detect_cycle on the same input correctly returns None.
confirmed_bugs.repro: python3 -c "import importlib.util as u; s=u.spec_from_file_location('d','tools/dag.py'); m=u.module_from_spec(s); s.loader.exec_module(m); print(m.critical_path({'nodes':[{'id':'A'},{'id':'B'}],'edges':[{'from':'A','to':'GHOST','kind':'depends'},{'from':'A','to':'B','kind':'depends'}]}))" -> KeyError: 'GHOST'. End-to-end: `dag render --file DAG.json` on a DAG with any dangling edge exits 1 with the same traceback, so the render tool cannot regenerate DAG.md for a corrupt graph.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Add the same membership guard to the edge loop: `for e in dag.get('edges', []): if e.get('kind','depends') != 'depends': continue; if e['from'] in indeg and e['to'] in indeg: adj[e['from']].append(e['to']); indeg[e['to']] += 1`. This makes critical_path tolerate dangling edges (treating them as absent, consistent with detect_cycle/descendants) and stops render from crashing on a corrupt DAG — turning the bricked file back into a verifiable/renderable one.

confirmed_bugs.title: workflow-run --dry is documented as "same as workflow-simulate" but is completely unimplemented — a --dry run performs a full real, side-effecting mutating run
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: AXON's CLI→state convention binds each flag to a working-memory key W:_<prog>-<flag> (verified: every program RETRIEVEs its own keys, e.g. W:_workflow-run-path, W:_workflow-run-name, W:_workflow-run-promote, W:_workflow-sim-* etc.). So `axon workflow-run --name X --dry` is parsed into W:_workflow-run-name=X AND W:_workflow-run-dry=true. The workflow-run body RETRIEVEs -path (line 55), -name (line 56), and -promote (line 44) but NEVER reads W:_workflow-run-dry — grep for `_workflow-run-dry` across the entire repo returns zero hits, and grep for 'dry' in workflow-run.md returns ONLY the HELP line 35 and the next-suggests metadata (line 12). There is no RETRIEVE(W:_workflow-run-dry), no IF-dry branch, no routing to workflow-simulate/simulate, and no side-effect suppression anywhere in the EXECUTE path or the kernel. The EXECUTE loop at line 89 calls `result ← EXEC({cursor.name})` unconditionally. The HELP block IS the canonical user-facing surface: help.md:58 parses `# usage:` lines straight out of ## HELP, so `axon help workflow-run` prints 'axon workflow-run --name <name> --dry — same as workflow-simulate' to the user. AXON is destructive-by-default: EXEC({cursor.name}) dispatches a synapse by name = program name, and shipped workflows contain real mutators — adaptive-free-text.yml has s2 `code-dev-flow` and s3 `code-dev-finalize` both tagged `role: mutator`, and mutator programs like code-dev-merge perform real writes (code-dev-merge.md:44 `REPLACE("{phase-dir}/_meta.md", "status: active", "status: merged")`). The compiled artifact that actually executes (dispatch.py:255 routes to workspace/programs/compiled/{prog}.cmp.md; run.py is the .cmp.md executor) ALSO advertises --dry at line 27 while its EXECUTE loop (line 59) is the same bare unconditional EXEC. Net: a user who runs `--dry` expecting workflow-simulate's behavior ('NO side-effects performed; this was a simulation', workflow-simulate.md:89) instead silently triggers a full mutating run — PRs marked merged, phases finalized, files rewritten.
confirmed_bugs.repro: axon workflow-run --name adaptive-free-text --dry  → the --dry flag is silently ignored; ACCEPTANCE-PREFLIGHT passes (acceptance not yet true), the EXECUTE loop runs, EXEC(code-dev-flow) and EXEC(code-dev-finalize) fire as real mutators (e.g. code-dev-merge's REPLACE rewrites _meta.md status active→merged), workflow-runner record-step writes trajectory files, and axon.workflow.completed is EMITted — all real side-effects, despite the user asking for a simulation. Contrast: `axon workflow-simulate --name adaptive-free-text` walks the same DAG with zero EXEC and prints '(NO side-effects performed; this was a simulation)'.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Implement the documented alias before the EXECUTE loop: after RESOLVE PATH, add `dry ← RETRIEVE(W:_workflow-run-dry) | ∅` and `IF dry ≠ ∅ → EXEC(workflow-simulate --path {path}) ; CLEAR(W:active-program) ; DONE(workflow-run)`. Recompile so workflow-run.cmp.md carries the branch. (If the alias is not going to be implemented, instead delete the `--dry` line from the ## HELP block in both workflow-run.md:35 and workflow-run.cmp.md:27 so the docs stop steering users into a real mutating run.)

confirmed_bugs.title: Branch-registry count is always +1 (header row counted) — dashboard shows '1 branch tracked' for an empty registry
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: `branches-count ← COUNT(EXTRACT(branches-content, "| ", mode=lines) WHERE line CONTAINS " | ") | 0`. Both scaffolders create 05-branches.md with a Markdown table whose HEADER row contains multiple ' | ' (space-pipe-space) separators: code-dev-init.md line 94 `| Branch | PR | Status | Last Commit | Commit Message |` and code-dev-new.md line 109 `| Branch | PR | Phase | Status | Notes |`. The separator row (`|--------|----|...`) has pipes adjacent to dashes, so it contains no ' | ' and is correctly excluded — but the header always matches the `CONTAINS " | "` predicate and is counted as one line. So branches-count = (real data rows) + 1 for every project. A brand-new registry with zero branches yields count 1, and the dashboard render at lines 394-396 prints '[✓] Branch registry: 1 branch(es) tracked' — a false positive.
confirmed_bugs.repro: `code-dev new` (or init) then `code-dev` (dashboard) on the fresh project → 'Branch registry: 1 branch(es) tracked' despite the table having no data rows. Add one real branch row → it reports 2.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Exclude the header: filter out the row whose cells are the literal column names (e.g. skip lines matching the header text / the line immediately preceding the `|---` separator), or count data rows as `total_pipe_rows - 2` (header+separator) clamped at 0.

confirmed_bugs.title: phase-ledger `verify` has no session-boundary fallback: with no literal 'start' row it scans ALL history, so stale prior-run rows satisfy a new run's expected phases
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: verify computes `last_start_ix` only by matching `phase.lower() == 'start'`; if a program never records a phase literally named `start`, `last_start_ix = -1` and `relevant = rows` (the entire history for that program). The docstring claims verification is 'since the most recent start OR session boundary' — but there is NO session-boundary detection (no PID/boot/timestamp gap logic). So a program that records only EXECUTE/DONE (R_PHASE_TRACKED merely requires SOME record call, not a start) will have a fresh, do-nothing run pass verify because a previous run's EXECUTE/DONE rows are still 'recorded'. The promised 'a previous run's done must not satisfy the new run' guarantee silently fails for any non-start-recording program.
confirmed_bugs.repro: append_row 'P','EXECUTE' then 'P','DONE' (prior run). New run records nothing. cmd_verify(program=P, expected='EXECUTE,DONE') prints ok:true and exits 0 — verified.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Fall back to a real session boundary when no 'start' row exists (e.g. split on session id / boot marker, or treat absence of a start as 'no completed window' → fail), and stop documenting a session-boundary fallback that isn't implemented.

confirmed_bugs.title: code-dev-plan auto-emit: build-from-prs lets a circular PR-dep crash the CLI; program expects a WARN, gets an uncaught exception after partial writes
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: build_from_prs() calls add_edge() per declared dependency (line 162); add_edge raises ValueError if a dep closes a cycle. cmd_build_from_prs (lines 619-624) does not catch it and calls _save_and_render only after build completes, so on a circular dep the ValueError propagates uncaught -> exit 1 + traceback, and nothing is written. But code-dev-plan.md line 371 wires this as best-effort: `IF build-r.ok ≢ true → LOG(WARN, ...)`, expecting a structured {ok:false} result. The tool never returns {ok:false} for this case — it crashes. Since the auto-emit runs AFTER 02-plan.md, 02-prs.md, phase files and _meta.md are already written (lines 292-351), a model that emits mutually-circular depends-on (e.g. PR-001 depends PR-002 and PR-002 depends PR-001) aborts the program post-write with a traceback instead of the intended warn-and-continue.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Bootstrap an empty plan DAG.json, then `dag build-from-prs --file DAG.json --prs-json '[{"number":"PR-001","title":"a","depends_on":"PR-002"},{"number":"PR-002","title":"b","depends_on":"PR-001"}]'`. Observed: uncaught ValueError (CYCLE_DETECTED), exit 1, no file written. Verified live.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In cmd_build_from_prs (or build_from_prs) catch the cycle, skip the offending edge or abort cleanly, and print {"ok": false, "code": "CYCLE_DETECTED", ...} with a non-zero-but-handled code so the program's WARN branch fires.

confirmed_bugs.title: plan_dag.py reports a phantom cycle when two PR files normalize to the same id
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _parse_pr derives the node id from path.stem.lower() (line 35). topo_sort builds by_id/indeg as dicts keyed by id, so two nodes with the same id collapse to one key, but `nodes` (the list) keeps both. Kahn compares `len(out) != len(nodes)` (line 68): out can contain at most one entry per distinct id, so with a duplicated id len(out) < len(nodes) even when the graph is perfectly acyclic, and the function returns `([], [remaining])` — a false CYCLE. run() then returns {"ok": False, "error": "cycle"} (line 143-144) and the reported cycle can even be the empty list [[]] (when all indegrees are 0). A genuine acyclic plan is rejected as cyclic with a nonsensical empty cycle.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Two PR files whose stems differ only by case/normalization (e.g. pr-001.md and PR-001.md on a case-sensitive FS) plus pr-002.md depending on pr-001 — i.e. nodes [{id:pr-001,deps:[]},{id:pr-001,deps:[]},{id:pr-002,deps:[pr-001]}]. topo_sort returns ([], [[]]) — phantom cycle. Verified live by calling topo_sort directly.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Dedup/detect collisions when building nodes (e.g. raise or merge on duplicate id), and compute the cycle/visited count against the distinct id set (len(by_id)) rather than len(nodes).

confirmed_bugs.title: rewind restores _meta + phases but NOT project-root state (04-log.md, 05-branches.md, _actions.log) → inconsistent, dangling-reference state after restore
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: save snapshots only `_meta.md` (line 80) and the `phases/` tree (line 81); rewind restores only those two (lines 65-68) and then APPENDs a 'tag rewind' line to the un-snapshotted 04-log.md (line 69). Project-root mutable state that other state programs depend on — 04-log.md (session markers + entries, read by resume/status/handoff/metrics), 05-branches.md (branch→PR registry, appended by journal-log), and _actions.log (the undo ledger) — is neither snapshotted nor restored. After tag→work→rewind, _meta.md and phases/ roll back to the tag era while 04-log.md / 05-branches.md / _actions.log still contain post-tag entries referencing PRs, branches, ADRs and phases that the reverted _meta.md+phases no longer contain. resume/metrics then read those orphaned rows (e.g. SESSION markers, branch rows, ADR-cite scans in metrics) producing dangling references and a self-inconsistent project.
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev tag v1; do work that appends to 04-log.md and adds a row to 05-branches.md and an _actions.log entry (e.g. code-dev log, code-dev pr); code-dev rewind v1 → _meta.md/phases revert but 04-log.md keeps the post-v1 SESSION/entry lines and 05-branches.md keeps the post-v1 branch row; `code-dev resume`/`code-dev status` now reference a phase/PR/branch the reverted meta doesn't know about.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Snapshot and restore 04-log.md, 05-branches.md, and _actions.log alongside _meta.md (and snapshot 04-log.md before the rewind append), or explicitly document/scope the snapshot and warn on restore that project-root logs are not reverted.

confirmed_bugs.title: pr-create never advances PR status in 02-prs.md (REPLACE is a guaranteed no-op)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: After writing the spec, pr-create does `REPLACE("…/02-prs.md", "**Status:** not-started  ← {pr-id}", "**Status:** spec-written")`. But code-dev-plan.md:311 (and compiled:158) writes every PR's status line as exactly `- **Status:** not-started\n` with NO `← {pr-id}` suffix (and no per-PR marker). The search literal `**Status:** not-started  ← PR-003` exists nowhere (grep across workspace/tests finds no such suffix). So the REPLACE matches nothing: 02-prs.md stays `not-started` forever after a spec is written. Downstream readers that surface PR status from 02-prs.md (and the user-facing list) stay stale; nothing flips a PR to spec-written there.
confirmed_bugs.repro: `code-dev plan` (writes `- **Status:** not-started`), then `code-dev pr 3`. Inspect 02-prs.md → PR-003 still shows `**Status:** not-started`; never becomes spec-written.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Match the actual line the planner writes and disambiguate by section: replace within the `## PR-003 — …` block, searching for `- **Status:** not-started` (no arrow), or key the replace on the per-PR Spec line.

confirmed_bugs.title: pr-sync output/EMIT dereference fields absent on skip/timeout/error paths
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: pr_sync.sync() returns NO pass/pending/fail keys on three branches: gh missing → `{ok:False, skipped:True, reason}` (pr_sync.py:12-13), timeout → `{ok:False, reason:'gh timeout'}` (18-19), non-JSON (24-25). But pr-sync.md unconditionally references `{result-json.pass}`, `{result-json.pending}`, `{result-json.fail}` in both the EMIT (line 40) and the `→ "✓ pr-… CI: …"` print (line 41) with no ok-check. On the very common 'gh not installed' / timeout path the program emits/prints empty or undefined fields and falsely shows a green '✓ CI' line, masking that no sync happened.
confirmed_bugs.repro: On a host without gh (or with gh slow >15s), run `code-dev pr sync 3`. sync() returns `{ok:False, skipped:True,…}`; pr-sync.md still prints `✓ pr-3 CI: ✓  pending ✗` (blank counts) and EMITs a synced event — a silent no-op disguised as success.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Guard on result-json.ok before printing/emitting: `IF result-json.ok ≠ true → report skipped/failed reason and DONE` else print the counts.

confirmed_bugs.title: check-only mode never propagates: gate sub-programs run in FULL mode (writes + user prompts) and never set their *-result keys
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: Preflight invokes its gate sub-programs as `EXEC(code-dev-review-scope, mode="check-only")` (L74), `EXEC(code-dev-review-self, mode="check-only", pr=pr)` (L92), `EXEC(code-dev-review-tests, mode="check-only", pr=pr)` (L117); pr-ready does the same for preflight (pr-ready L64). But each callee reads its mode ONLY from a specifically-named W: key — review-scope L40 `RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-scope-check-mode)`, review-self L42 `RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-self-review-mode)`, review-tests L38 `RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-suggest-tests-mode)`, preflight L46 `RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-preflight-mode)` — and NO program anywhere STOREs any of those keys (grep across workspace/programs finds zero writers). The neuron-contract binds program inputs via explicit `w-key:` (NEURON-CONTRACT L151-159); there is no rule mapping a literal EXEC arg named `mode` onto a differently-named W: key. So every callee defaults `mode ← … | "full"`. In full mode the check-only short-circuits (review-self L80-83, review-scope L59-62, review-tests L64-67, preflight L147-150) are skipped, so the callee NEVER stores `W:code-dev-self-review-result` / `…-scope-check-result` / etc. Preflight then reads `selfrev.gaps`, `scope.scope-clean`, `test-suggest.coverage` off a value that lacks those fields, and — worse — each callee runs its FULL body inside the gate: review-self/tests MKDIR+WRITE report files, and review-self/diff/tests call `QUERY(user): "Which PR?"` when pr is unset (review-self L41), i.e. a 'check-only' gate performs disk writes and blocks on interactive prompts. The entire check-only contract these gates depend on is unreachable as written.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Run `code-dev preflight`. review-self executes its full path: writes `phases/{phase}/03-prs/{pr}/self-review.md` and (if no pr) prompts the user — neither expected from a gate — and `W:code-dev-self-review-result` is never set, so Gate 4 reads `selfrev.gaps` from a fieldless return.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Have callers set the W: key the callee actually reads before EXEC, e.g. `STORE(W:code-dev-self-review-mode,"check-only")` + `STORE(W:code-dev-pr-create,pr)` then `EXEC(code-dev-review-self)`; clear afterward. Apply for scope/self/tests/preflight.

confirmed_bugs.title: promote round-trip is broken: run records cursor.id but promote treats node value as the synapse NAME, yielding non-executable drafts (and duplicate names on cyclic runs)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The EXECUTE loop persists each step with `--node {cursor.id}` (workflow-run.md:92), so the trajectory stores ids: s1, s2, s3. promote() (workflow_run.py:78-86) then builds each draft synapse as `{'id': f's{i+1}', 'name': n}` where n is the stored node value — i.e. it puts the OLD ID into the `name` field. The produced draft has synapses named 's1','s2','s3' (verified). But a real run dispatches by name: EXEC({cursor.name}) (workflow-run.md:89). So re-running a promoted draft executes EXEC('s1'), EXEC('s2'), ... which are synapse IDs, not real program/synapse names like 'code-dev-study' — the promoted 'fixed workflow' is non-executable. This also internally contradicts the trace built at line 90, which uses cursor.name. Worse, promote only collapses CONSECUTIVE duplicates (workflow_run.py:80), so an adaptive run that legitimately revisits a node (trajectory s1,s2,s1,s2,s3) promotes to five synapses with names ['s1','s2','s1','s2','s3'] — duplicate names and a linear chain that erases the cycle (verified).
confirmed_bugs.repro: Run an adaptive workflow so record-step writes cursor.id per step (s1,s2,s3), then `workflow-run --promote <run-id>` (or call promote on the persisted trajectory). The draft's synapses have name: s1/s2/s3. Adopt and run it: every step does EXEC('sN') against a non-existent synapse. For a revisiting run (s1,s2,s1,s2,s3) the draft additionally contains duplicate synapse names.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Record the synapse NAME in the trajectory (--node {cursor.name}) — or store both and have promote read step['name']; and de-duplicate by full set (reuse one synapse id per distinct node) so cycles don't fan out into duplicate-named linear steps.

confirmed_bugs.title: Non-list `on-complete` silently bypasses dangling-next AND reachability checks — dag returns ok=true on a graph with a dangling reference
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: In the dangling-next pass, `rules = syn.get("on-complete") or []` then `if not isinstance(rules, list): continue` (lines 188-190) skips the entire node when on-complete is e.g. a dict. In _reachable_from, `for rule in rules:` over a dict iterates its KEYS (strings) and `if not isinstance(rule, dict): continue` (lines 80-82) drops them all, so the node is treated as terminal. Net: a malformed on-complete makes dangling targets invisible AND the node looks like a dead-end, so the referenced target is reported (if at all) only as a phantom 'orphan', never as the real dangling-next/schema-error, and a single-node file returns ok=true.
confirmed_bugs.repro: notlist.yml: synapse a with `on-complete:` being the mapping `{next: ghost}` (not a list). `python3 tools/workflow_dag.py analyze` -> ok:True, defects:[] — the dangling 'ghost' is completely missed. Verified _reachable_from('a',{a:{on-complete:{next:ghost}},ghost:...}) returns {'a'} only.
confirmed_bugs.fix: When on-complete is present but not a list, emit a schema-error defect instead of `continue`; have _reachable_from likewise guard `isinstance(rules, list)` and flag non-list as a defect rather than silently treating it as terminal.

confirmed_bugs.title: Pass-validate-fail-run: schema makes synapse `name` optional but run dispatches EXEC({cursor.name}) — a name-less synapse validates clean then dispatches a null program
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The schema's synapse definition requires only `id` (workflow-file.schema.json line 98: "required": ["id"]); `name` is optional. workflow_dag.py never checks for name. But workflow-run.md line 89 does `result <- EXEC({cursor.name})` and line 88 records `--note '{cursor.name}'`; simulate prints `{cursor.id} -> {cursor.name}` (blank). A schema-valid, dag-clean workflow whose synapse omits `name` therefore passes both validators and then, at run time, dispatches the empty/null program name (EXEC(∅)).
confirmed_bugs.repro: noname.yml: synapse `- id: a` with `on-complete: []` and no `name`. Confirmed dag analyze -> ok:True and it is schema-valid. workflow-run.md then executes EXEC({cursor.name}) with name unset.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Add a defect in workflow_dag.analyze() (and a semantic check in workflow-validate.md) for any synapse missing a non-empty `name`, OR make `name` required in the schema's synapse.required.

confirmed_bugs.title: workflow-validate returns `errors` as a LIST but run/simulate/edit compare it to an integer (v.errors == 0 / v.errors > 0)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: workflow-validate.md sets `errors` to a list (it APPENDs strings, line 51/55/58) and returns `DONE(workflow-validate, { ok: ok, errors: errors, warnings: warnings })` (line 79) — errors is the list, with the COUNT only used internally for `ok` (line 64). But every caller treats v.errors as a count: workflow-run.md line 62 `ASSERT(v.errors ≡ 0)`, workflow-simulate.md line 43 `ASSERT(v.errors ≡ 0)`, workflow-edit.md line 89 `IF v.errors > 0`. Comparing a non-empty list to 0 is a type-confused guard: depending on interpreter coercion it either never trips (list != 0 is always true so `≡ 0` is false even when there ARE errors -> invalid workflows slip past run/simulate's preflight) or errors out. The contract field name 'errors' is overloaded between list-of-strings and a count.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Author any workflow with a dangling next so validate returns ok:false, errors:['synapse a: on-complete.next=...']. run/simulate evaluate `v.errors ≡ 0`: a 1-element list is not equal to integer 0, so the guard does not see '0 errors' nor a clean '> 0' count — the preflight relies on coercion that doesn't match the returned shape, so the schema-fail gate is unreliable.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Have validate's DONE return a count alongside the list (e.g. error-count: COUNT(errors)) and change callers to test `v.ok ≡ false` or `v.error-count > 0`, not `v.errors`.

confirmed_bugs.title: Any unknown `code-dev phase <typo>` interpolates into a non-existent program — silent dead route with no clean failure
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: Because line 96 interpolates the raw subcommand into the program name with no allow-list and no default-to-error, any unrecognized phase subcommand becomes EXEC(code-dev-phase-<typo>), which resolves to nothing. There is no `ELSE → FAIL(...)` guarding the phase verb, so a mistyped subcommand neither runs a program nor emits a structured FAIL with a fix/suggested_next — it just dead-ends. This is the same class as the confirmed `code-dev new` dead-route but for the open-ended phase namespace. The static resolution test that would normally catch broken EXEC targets (tests/test_code_dev_dispatch.py::test_code_dev_exec_targets_resolve) explicitly EXCLUDES interpolated targets — see its docstring line 15 ("Dynamic/interpolated targets (e.g. EXEC(code-dev-phase-{...})) are excluded by design") and the _NAME_RE filter at line 44 that skips any first-token containing '{'. I ran it: it passes (1 passed) even though this route is broken — confirming the blind spot: no automated check guards line 96.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Load a project, run `code-dev phase satus` (typo for status) or `code-dev phase lst`. Line 96 → EXEC(code-dev-phase-satus) → no such program → silent unresolved route; the user gets no error, no fix, no list. Meanwhile pytest tests/test_code_dev_dispatch.py::test_code_dev_exec_targets_resolve stays green.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Replace the interpolated EXEC with an explicit dispatch over a known set and a trailing guard: `... ELSE → FAIL(code-dev, problem="Unknown phase subcommand '{phase-sub}'", fix="Use: code-dev phase new | start | list", suggested_next="code-dev status,code-dev phase new")`. Separately, drop the interpolation exclusion in test_code_dev_dispatch (or add a dedicated test) so dynamic phase targets are resolution-checked against the on-disk allow-list.

confirmed_bugs.title: merge_nodes / split_node / fold_in mutate edges with no cycle or dangling guard, so they can persist a cyclic or dangling DAG that bypasses CYCLE_DETECTED
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: add_edge has a cycle guard (line 133: rejects a depends-edge that closes a cycle) and cmd_add_edge re-checks detect_cycle BEFORE _save_and_render (lines 609-614, returning code 3 without writing). But the redirect-style mutators do not. merge_nodes redirects edge endpoints to `into` (247-249) and only drops edges that were internal to the merged set (243-245); a surviving edge can now point at a non-node or form a cycle. fold_in moves child_id's edges onto `into` (283-286) and only removes resulting self-loops (287, `e['from'] != e['to']`) — but a two-hop cycle like A->C plus C->A is NOT a self-loop and survives. split_node duplicates incoming/outgoing edges to every target (270-274) with no acyclicity check. None of these call detect_cycle (grep confirms detect_cycle appears only at 133, 382, 609), and their cmd_* wrappers (cmd_merge 655, cmd_split 665, cmd_fold_in 673) go straight to _save_and_render. So they can commit a cyclic DAG to disk with exit 0 (no crash, because critical_path returns [] for a pure cycle at line 341 rather than raising).
