The DAG.md is then silently rendered without a critical-path line, and only a later `dag verify` flags CYCLE_DETECTED — the guarantee that mutations preserve acyclicity (enforced for add-edge) is silently violated for merge/split/fold-in. Verified: `fold-in --child-id B --into A` on A->B,B->C,C->A writes nodes ['A','C'], edges [('A','C'),('C','A')] and exits 0; verify then reports CYCLE_DETECTED.
confirmed_bugs.repro: DAG with nodes A,B,C and depends edges A->B, B->C, C->A. Run `python3 tools/dag.py fold-in --file DAG.json --child-id B --into A`. Exits 0 (`{"ok": true, ...}`), DAG.json on disk now has a cycle A<->C, DAG.md is regenerated with no critical-path. The cycle was committed despite add-edge-level cycle protection elsewhere. (Same class: a merge whose redirect creates a cycle, or whose surviving edge dangles.)
confirmed_bugs.fix: After mutating edges in merge_nodes/split_node/fold_in (or once, centrally, in _save_and_render before the write), run detect_cycle(dag) and verify-for-dangling; if either fails, raise/return an error and do NOT write. Cleanest: have _save_and_render call a pre-write `verify(dag)` (or at least detect_cycle + dangling scan) and abort the _atomic_write on any error-level issue, so no mutator can persist a cyclic/dangling graph — mirroring the cmd_add_edge gate at lines 609-614 for all mutators.

confirmed_bugs.title: journal-decision writes op=append with a snapshot-DIR path, but undo's append branch truncates to that field as a byte size — undo corrupts/errors instead of restoring
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: journal-decision takes a FULL-FILE snapshot of _decisions.md (COPY-FILE at line 66) and then records the action as: APPEND(_actions.log, "{ts.iso}  {action-id}  append  {decisions-path}  {snap-dir}/_decisions.md\n") (lines 67-68). The op is 'append' and the 5th field is a file path. But the undo consumer's 'append' branch (state-undo.md line 79) does TRUNCATE(target-path, size=snap-path) where it explicitly expects snap-path to be a numeric pre-append byte size (HELP line 30 + comment line 77: 'snap-path holds the pre-append size'). Here snap-path is the string "{snap-dir}/_decisions.md", not a number, so the truncate is fed a non-numeric size. Full-file snapshots must use op 'write'/'replace' (state-undo lines 72-75 COPY-FILE the snapshot back); op 'append' has no restore-from-file path. So undo of an ADR either errors or truncates _decisions.md to a garbage/zero length — destroying prior ADRs instead of removing just the last one.
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev decision "X" (writes ADR-002 appended to a _decisions.md that already has ADR-001), then code-dev undo. The last actions.log row is '...  append  .../_decisions.md  .../archive/snapshots/act-.../_decisions.md'. undo parses op=append, snap-path=the file path, and runs TRUNCATE(_decisions.md, size='.../_decisions.md') — truncation to a path string, not restoring ADR-001 from the snapshot dir.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In journal-decision record op 'replace' (not 'append') so undo COPY-FILEs the snapshot back: APPEND(_actions.log, "{ts.iso} {action-id} replace {decisions-path} {snap-dir}/_decisions.md\n").

confirmed_bugs.title: workflow-new always emits a dangling on-complete.next on the LAST synapse, so PHASE-E validation rejects every workflow it builds
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: In the PHASE-C build loop, EVERY appended synapse is given a hard-coded forward edge: `synapses ← APPEND(synapses, { id: "s{step}", name: picked, on-complete: [{next: "s{step+1}"}] })`. There is no terminal handling — when the user types 'done' the loop just BREAKs (line 92), leaving the final synapse sN still pointing at next:s{N+1}, which is never created. PHASE-E then runs `EXEC(workflow-validate --path {out-path})` (line 133) and aborts if errors>0 (line 137). workflow-validate.md lines 50-51 explicitly flag any on-complete.next not in synapse-ids: "on-complete.next='sN+1' is not a known synapse id". So the author's own mandatory validation gate rejects the file. Net effect: workflow-new can NEVER successfully save any workflow that contains at least one synapse — it always FAILs at PHASE-E with a dangling-edge error the user did not introduce.
confirmed_bugs.repro: axon workflow-new -> name 'demo', domain 'code-dev', mode 'fixed', a goal, pick exactly one synapse (e.g. code-dev-study), type 'done'. Draft has synapses=[{id:s1,name:code-dev-study,on-complete:[{next:s2}]}] with no s2. workflow-validate returns 1 error -> FAIL(workflow-new, 'Workflow draft failed schema validation'). The file is written to disk (line 131, before validation) but the program reports failure and never EMITs axon.workflow.authored.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Don't pre-wire next on append; after the loop set each synapse's on-complete to [{next:'s{i+1}'}] for i<N and [] (terminal) for the last, e.g. mark sN.on-complete = [] before building the draft.

confirmed_bugs.title: R_MEMORY_RESPECTED is dead in the crucible changeset gate (declared BLOCK + manifest-locked into the gate, but always no-ops because run_changeset never supplies program_text)
confirmed_bugs.line: 152-160 (ctx build + rule loop); rule at tools/rules/r_memory_respected.py:57-61
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: run_changeset builds `ctx = {"changed_files": ..., "repo_root": cwd}` and then calls r_memory_respected.check(ctx) (crucible.py:152-156). But R_MEMORY_RESPECTED.check does `text = ctx.get("program_text"); if not text: return None` (r_memory_respected.py:58-60). The changeset ctx has NO program_text key, so the rule returns None on every invocation — it can never emit a violation. This is the rule's PRIMARY block-enforcement surface: manifest.py:40 lists r_memory_respected under runners ['crucible','lint','audit'] (NOT 'verify'), and manifest.py:6 defines the 'crucible' runner as `crucible.run_changeset() -> the merge changeset gate`. crucible.py's module docstring (line 7) and the changeset-rules control note in crucible.json ('R_NEW_NEEDS_TEST + R_MEMORY_RESPECTED over the diff') both assert this rule gates merges. A parity test (test_rules_manifest) even LOCKS r_memory_respected into the crucible runner — so the rule is declared BLOCK, locked into the gate, and silently inert. A program added in a diff that does WRITE(workspace/memory/general/foo) (bypassing agent-memory) passes `crucible gate` clean.
confirmed_bugs.repro: 1) Add a program file in the diff containing a line like `WRITE("workspace/memory/programs/x.md", ...)`. 2) Run `python3 tools/crucible.py changeset` (or `crucible gate`, which runs the changeset-rules control). 3) Observe ok:true / no R_MEMORY_RESPECTED violation, even though the rule's regex _TIER_WRITE_RE matches that text — because run_changeset fed it changed_files only, never the file's program_text.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In run_changeset, for each changed program/tool path read its text and run r_memory_respected per-file with ctx including program_text/program_path (mirroring how lint_summary.py:91 and neuron_audit.py:44 build the ctx), rather than passing the changeset-only ctx that the rule cannot consume.

confirmed_bugs.title: journal-log undo snapshot records pre-size AFTER the append already ran — TRUNCATE becomes a no-op, so undo silently fails to remove the log entry
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The schema mandates snapshotting size BEFORE mutating (_code-dev-schema-v4.md lines 127-131: 'pre-size ← FILE-SIZE(target) ... # then mutate'). journal-log does the opposite: line 204 APPEND(log-file, log-entry) mutates first, then line 207 pre-size ← FILE-SIZE(log-file) reads the size AFTER the append, then line 209 records that (already-grown) size as the 'pre-append' snapshot. On undo, state-undo.md line 79 runs TRUNCATE(log-file, size=pre-size) with pre-size == current size, so it truncates the file to its own length — a no-op. The just-written entry is never reversed, and undo prints '✓ Truncated ... to N bytes' (false success).
confirmed_bugs.repro: code-dev log → fill fields → confirm 'yes' (entry appended to 04-log.md, actions.log row records the POST-append size). Then code-dev undo → confirm 'yes' → '✓ Truncated 04-log.md to {size} bytes' but the entry is still present.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Move the size capture before the append: compute pre-size ← IF FILE-EXISTS(log-file) → FILE-SIZE(log-file) | 0 ABOVE line 204, then APPEND, then record pre-size.

confirmed_bugs.title: replay <PR-N>: the '| rounds' fallback makes a missing-PR query display a DIFFERENT PR's reviewer rounds (wrong cross-reference)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: Line 43 builds rounds = SCAN("round-*-{pr}.md") + SCAN("round-*.md"); the second glob pulls EVERY round file in the phase regardless of PR. Line 44: pr-rounds ← FILTER(rounds, contains=pr) | rounds. When the phase has review rounds for other PRs but none for the requested {pr}, FILTER(contains=pr) returns empty, so the '| rounds' fallback fires and pr-rounds becomes ALL rounds in that phase. Line 45 then sets found = {phase, rounds: <those unrelated rounds>}, the ASSERT(found ≠ ∅) passes, and the program renders another PR's reviewer summaries under the header 'code-dev replay {pr}'. The reverse-lookup that should have reported 'no rounds for {pr}' instead silently attributes foreign rounds to {pr}. (Secondary: line 45 uses '=' so when a PR's rounds span multiple phases only the last phase's rounds survive — earlier ones are dropped.)
confirmed_bugs.repro: Phase 3-implement/reviews has round-1-PR-003.md but no PR-007 file. Run code-dev replay PR-007. FILTER(contains='PR-007') over [..., round-1-PR-003.md] is empty → fallback → pr-rounds = [round-1-PR-003.md] → output shows PR-003's review summary labeled as PR-007.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Drop the '| rounds' fallback (use pr-rounds ← FILTER(rounds, contains=pr)) and accumulate across phases (found ← found + {...}) instead of overwriting with '='.

confirmed_bugs.title: workflow-run ignores wf.start and always begins at synapses[0]; edit can reorder/repoint start and the run silently executes from the wrong node
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The runner's entry point is `cursor ← wf.synapses[0]` (line 79). `wf.start` is never read anywhere in workflow-run.md (grep shows 'start' only in a phase-ledger note and comments). The schema makes `start` a REQUIRED field (workflow-file.schema.json line 7) and WORKFLOW-FILE.md §Validation item 2 says 'start references a real id', but the executor authoritatively uses array order instead. So `start` and the actual run-entry can diverge with zero diagnostics. workflow-edit choice (c) 'add/remove/rename' mutates wf.synapses (line 77) and re-serializes via WRITE-YAML (line 87); any reorder, or prepending a new first synapse, changes synapses[0] without touching start. workflow-validate never cross-checks start vs synapses[0] (semantic block lines 46-58 omits start entirely), so it passes, and the run begins at a node the authored graph says is NOT the start.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Take authored-python-code-dev.yml (start:s1, synapses s1..s5). workflow-edit -> (c) synapses -> add a new prep synapse inserted at the head of the list (or rename/reorder so a non-s1 ends up first). Save: validate passes (no dup, all on-complete.next resolve). workflow-run --name python-code-dev: cursor=synapses[0]=the new/other node, NOT s1. The advance-guard (line 151) only checks declared on-complete edges from the actual cursor, so a wrong-but-edge-consistent start runs the corrupted graph to completion and EMITs axon.workflow.completed as if normal.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Set cursor from start: `cursor ← FIND(wf.synapses, id ≡ wf.start) | wf.synapses[0]` and FAIL if not found; equivalently have workflow-validate assert start ∈ synapse-ids.

confirmed_bugs.title: Validator never checks `start` references a real id, has no reachability/orphan check, and no cycle check — edit can orphan/strand nodes or delete the start target with validate still green
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The semantic block validates only three things: on-complete.next ∈ synapse-ids (50-51), duplicate ids (53-55), and zero synapses (57-58). It does NOT validate that `start` ∈ synapse-ids, does NOT compute reachability from start (orphaned/unreachable synapses pass), and does NOT run the acyclic/Kahn cycle check that WORKFLOW-FILE.md §Validation items 2,3 require. The JSON schema is no backstop: start is just {type:string,minLength:1} (schema line 85) and on-complete.next is just a string (line 128) with no referential constraint. So workflow-edit's save gate (lines 88-91: 'IF v.errors>0 revert') is satisfied by structurally-broken graphs.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Edit authored-python-code-dev.yml -> (c) synapses -> remove s1 (the start target) but leave start:s1. validate: start unchecked, no dangling on-complete.next (nothing pointed back to s1), no orphan check -> v.errors==0 -> save succeeds, backup overwritten with the broken file. Now start:s1 dangles; on next workflow-run line 79 takes synapses[0]=s2 instead, masking the corruption. Variant: remove a mid-chain node referenced only as a start/entry of an isolated subgraph, or add a synapse with on-complete:[] that nothing points to -> permanently orphaned, validate green.
confirmed_bugs.fix: In the semantic block add: IF payload.start NOT IN synapse-ids -> error; compute reachable set from start over on-complete.next and error/warn on unreachable ids; run the plan_dag Kahn cycle detector and error on cycles (per spec items 2,3).

confirmed_bugs.title: Corrupted-but-valid-JSON rollback sidecar crashes every L: write (wedges dev-mode/halt-mode toggle)
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: write_longterm_value loads the rollback sidecar with json.load inside a try/except (lines 66-70) that ONLY guards against parse errors — it sets history=[] only when json.load raises. If the sidecar is valid JSON but not a list (a string, dict, or int — e.g. it was truncated/overwritten/hand-edited to '"x"' or '{}'), json.load succeeds and returns that non-list, then line 71 `history.insert(0, old)` raises AttributeError ('str'/'dict' object has no attribute 'insert'). The exception propagates out of write_longterm_value before the actual value is ever written (line 73 never runs), so the L: key is left at its OLD value and the whole write aborts. Confirmed live: with workspace/memory/longterm/.rollback/dev-mode.json = '"corrupted"', calling write_from_workspace(ws,'dev-mode','false') raises AttributeError and dev-mode.md stays 'true' — the security write-gate is stuck ON and cannot be toggled OFF via memory.py/session_save (both route through this single writer).
confirmed_bugs.repro: echo true > workspace/memory/longterm/dev-mode.md; mkdir -p workspace/memory/longterm/.rollback; echo '"oops"' > workspace/memory/longterm/.rollback/dev-mode.json; python3 tools/memory.py set --scope L --key dev-mode --value false  →  AttributeError, dev-mode remains true.
confirmed_bugs.fix: After json.load, coerce non-list to []: `if not isinstance(history, list): history = []` (or widen the except to also reset on TypeError/AttributeError).

confirmed_bugs.title: Canonical L: reader silently corrupts any value containing a 'value:' line — flips dev-mode/halt-mode
confirmed_bugs.mechanism:
read_longterm_value scans EVERY line of the file (lines 23-25) and, on the first line whose stripped/lowered form startswith('value:'), returns only that line's tail. But the single L: writer (write_longterm_value, line 73) and memory.py set (line 59) write the raw value verbatim as `value + '
'` with NO 'value:' front-matter and NO escaping. So the reader's 'value:' branch never fires on legitimately-written data — it only ever fires on a content collision, where it discards the real stored value. Because this is THE canonical reader used by enforce.py (dev-mode gate), verify.py (halt-mode), _axon_io._dev_mode_active (in-process write-gate), and boot.py, a value such as 'false
value: true' written to L:dev-mode is read back as 'true'. Confirmed live: write_from_workspace(ws,'dev-mode','false
value: true') makes _dev_mode_active() return True — the write-gate state diverges from the value the owner actually set. Single-line scalars (the common case) are unaffected, but the read is unsound for any multi-line / colon-bearing value (e.g. last-session-summary, or any user-supplied --value via memory.py set).
confirmed_bugs.repro: python3 tools/memory.py set --scope L --key dev-mode --value $'false\nvalue: true'; python3 tools/enforce.py check-write --target axon/x.md  →  reports dev_mode:true and ALLOWS the axon/ write, though the owner set dev-mode to 'false'.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Drop the whole-file 'value:' scan; either return raw.strip() (writer is always bare form), or restrict the front-matter parse to the FIRST line only.

confirmed_bugs.title: verify status misreports enforcement posture: claims runtime WARN fails the gate under strict halt-mode, but the gate (_run) ignores halt-mode and passes all WARNs
confirmed_bugs.line: gate: 163 (passed = not blocks); status report: 236 + note 240
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _run computes `passed = not blocks` where `blocks = [v for v in violations if v.severity == 'BLOCK']` (verify.py:156,163). halt_mode is read (line 155) and echoed in output but NEVER used to escalate WARN -> the comment at 159-162 makes this explicit ('WARN is genuinely non-fatal regardless of halt-mode'). However cmd_status reports `"warn_is_block": halt != "soft"` with the inline comment 'under strict, a runtime WARN already fails the kernel gate' (line 236), and the note (line 240) states 'a runtime WARN fails the gate unless halt-mode=soft'. Under the DEFAULT halt-mode=strict these are flatly contradicted by _run: a runtime WARN does NOT fail the gate. cmd_status exists specifically to give operators a truthful single-view enforcement posture (F17, docstring lines 214-217); it now reports the opposite of the gate's actual behavior, so an operator reading `warn_is_block:true` believes a WARN-severity runtime rule (e.g. an advisory) will block a response when it will not.
confirmed_bugs.repro: With halt-mode unset/strict: `python3 tools/verify.py status` prints `"warn_is_block": true` and the note saying runtime WARNs fail the gate. Then craft an output that trips only a WARN-severity runtime rule and run `python3 tools/verify.py output --text '...'`: result is passed:true, exit 0 (WARN did not block). The two commands disagree about whether WARN blocks.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Make status reflect reality: set `warn_is_block = False` (WARN never blocks post-F15/F16) and rewrite the note, OR conversely re-introduce halt-mode escalation in _run if blocking-on-strict is the intended contract — pick one and make gate and status agree.

confirmed_bugs.title: Dispatch prefer-compiled bypasses the confidence threshold on pure noise — any best_score > 0 dispatches, so a single incidental shared word routes to a near-random program
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: When preferences set prefer-compiled=true, the threshold is bypassed for ANY positive similarity: `effective_match = best_score >= threshold or (prefer_compiled and best_score > 0)`. TF-IDF cosine gives a non-trivial positive score from a single shared non-stopword token (verified: query 'deploy the kubernetes cluster' vs program desc 'the cluster of bananas' -> 0.2867). With prefer-compiled on, that 0.2867 (or even 0.05 from one rare shared token) clears `> 0` and the code emits action:dispatch + a `run: EXEC(.../<best_prog>.cmp.md)` instruction and stamps W:_last-dispatch — routing the user to a program that merely shares one incidental word, far below the operator's configured dispatch-confidence (default 0.65). prefer-compiled was meant to prefer a compiled variant of a genuinely-matched program, not to disable matching entirely.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Set `prefer-compiled: true` in workspace/preferences/smart-dispatch.md. Populate the index with one program whose description shares a single common word with an unrelated query. Run `python3 tools/dispatch.py match --query '<unrelated prompt sharing one word>'`. Output is action:dispatch with confidence ~0.05-0.3 (< 0.65 threshold) and a run/EXEC directive, instead of fallback.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Gate prefer-compiled on a meaningful floor, e.g. `best_score >= max(threshold*K, MIN_FLOOR)` or only let prefer-compiled relax the threshold by a bounded delta — never accept bare `> 0`.

confirmed_bugs.title: Cron default job 'deprecation-log cron' fails every tick (--workspace placed before subcommand on a tool whose --workspace is subparser-local), trips the breaker, and permanently self-disables
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _build_job_cmd assumes --workspace is a GLOBAL flag on every AXON tool and so emits it right after the tool name, BEFORE the subcommand: ['deprecation-log','--workspace',WS,'cron','--lookahead-days','14']. axon.py (line 95) is a pure passthrough, so deprecation_log.py receives '--workspace WS cron ...'. But deprecation_log.py calls add_subparsers FIRST (line 336) and defines --workspace only on each SUBPARSER (line 349+). argparse therefore treats '--workspace' as the 'action' positional choice and errors: "argument action: invalid choice: '<ws>' (choose from record,list,due,cron,sweep)", exit 2. The seeded default job 'axon-deprecation-cron' (cron.py:427) thus returns ok=False on every tick. _apply_breaker increments consecutive_failures; after BREAKER_DEFAULT_N=3 ticks (3 boots) it flips enabled=False and the weekly deprecation-sunset surface is silently, permanently dead.
confirmed_bugs.repro: python3 -c "import sys;sys.path.insert(0,'tools');import cron;print(cron._run_job({'program':'deprecation-log cron --lookahead-days 14','id':'X'},'/tmp/ws'))"  -> (False, 'usage: deprecation_log.py ... error: argument action: invalid choice'). Run cron 'tick --auto' 3 times -> job auto-disabled by circuit breaker.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Detect subparser-local --workspace tools (or just append --workspace at the END for CLI form, after the subcommand) — e.g. build [tool, *parts[1:], '--workspace', ws]; for the truly-global ones either placement works, for subparser-local ones only the trailing placement parses.

confirmed_bugs.title: Cron default job 'dispatch-stats weekly' invokes a non-existent action ('weekly'); the job has never run successfully and trips the breaker
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The seeded default is ('axon-dispatch-stats', 'dispatch-stats weekly', 'weekly Mon 09:10', ...). But dispatch_stats.py defines its positional as choices=['summary','savings','precision'] (line 120) — there is no 'weekly' action. So _run_job builds 'axon.py dispatch-stats --workspace WS weekly', dispatch_stats.py rejects the positional 'weekly' with exit 2 ("invalid choice: 'weekly'"). The job returns ok=False on every tick from first boot; after 3 ticks _apply_breaker disables it. The weekly dispatch token-savings summary never fires. (Distinct root cause from the --workspace-ordering bug: here --workspace placement is actually fine because dispatch_stats's --workspace is top-level; the command verb itself is wrong.)
confirmed_bugs.repro: python3 -c "import sys;sys.path.insert(0,'tools');import cron;print(cron._run_job({'program':'dispatch-stats weekly','id':'X'},'/tmp/ws'))"  -> (False, "usage: dispatch_stats.py ... {summary,savings,precision}"). Equivalent: python3 axon.py dispatch-stats --workspace /tmp/ws weekly ; echo $?  -> 2.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Change the default to a real action, e.g. 'dispatch-stats summary' (or 'savings') in cron.py:412.

confirmed_bugs.title: journal-log 'Open PRs' filter keys on a 'status: complete' marker that log entries never contain — completed PRs are never filtered out
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: Line 108: logged-done ← EXTRACT(READ(log-file), "status: complete", mode=pr-ids). Line 109: open-prs ← all-prs WHERE pr-id NOT IN logged-done. But the log entries this program writes (lines 179-190) only ever emit the fields 'pr:', 'files:', and 'drift:' — never the string 'status: complete' (a grep across all programs shows the only writer of 'status: complete' is migrate-workspace.md, not the journal). Therefore logged-done is always empty and open-prs == all-prs on every run; the interactive 'Open PRs:' list (lines 111-113) keeps showing PRs that have already been fully logged, defeating the filter's entire purpose.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Log multiple entries for PR-001 until it is effectively done, then run code-dev log again: PR-001 still appears under 'Open PRs:' because no entry ever wrote 'status: complete'.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Either have the writer stamp a completion marker (e.g. write 'status: complete' when the user marks a PR done) or change line 108 to derive done-PRs from an existing source (e.g. merged PRs in _events.log 'pr-merged' rows).

confirmed_bugs.title: accountability _prune keeps the OLDEST-reconciled entries and silently drops the most-recently-reconciled ones — opposite of its documented contract
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: _prune returns opened + done[-RECONCILED_KEEP:]. The slice keeps the last 25 by LIST POSITION, and entries are appended (cmd_open line 66) in open-creation order, never re-ordered on reconcile (cmd_reconcile only flips status in place, line 79-83). So 'most recent RECONCILED_KEEP reconciled ones' (docstring line 58) is false: retention is by open-order, not by reconciled_at. When >25 reconciled entries exist, reconciling an entry that was opened EARLY (low index) drops it even though it was just reconciled (freshest), while an entry opened late but reconciled long ago survives. This silently loses the newest audit records — exactly the ones a Stop-hook reviewer would care about.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Open R0..R29 in order, then reconcile in reverse (R29 first ... R0 last). Result kept = {R5..R29}; R0..R4 (the 5 MOST-recently reconciled) are dropped, R29 (oldest reconciliation) is kept. Verified: python3 driver opening 30 then reconciling 29..0 -> load_ledger shows refs [5..29], R0 absent, R29 present.
confirmed_bugs.fix: Sort done by its reconciled_at timestamp before slicing: done = sorted(done, key=lambda e: e.get('reconciled_at',''))[-RECONCILED_KEEP:].

confirmed_bugs.title: cron breaker-status raises AttributeError when a job has disabled_reason set to JSON null
confirmed_bugs.mechanism: The filter is `if j.get("disabled_reason", "").startswith("circuit-breaker")`. The "" default only applies when the KEY is absent; if the key exists with value null (None) — which is valid JSON and survives _read round-trips — .get returns None and None.startswith raises AttributeError, crashing the entire breaker-status action (uncaught) before any output is printed. cron.json is hand-editable and a half-written/normalized record can carry disabled_reason: null.
confirmed_bugs.repro: Put a job with "disabled_reason": null into workspace/scheduler/cron.json, then run: python3 axon.py cron breaker-status --workspace <ws>  -> traceback: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'startswith'. Minimal: python3 -c "print({'disabled_reason':None}.get('disabled_reason','').startswith('x'))".
confirmed_bugs.fix: Use `(j.get("disabled_reason") or "").startswith("circuit-breaker")` on line 523.

dont_do_enforcement_findings.title: Write-time hook cannot find the project's _dont-do.md when editing codebase source (walk-up never crosses into my-axon/dev-projects)
dont_do_enforcement_findings.mechanism: The hook claims to be the keystroke guard that makes tokenized dont-dos "ALWAYS enforced across the project" (file header, lines 8-10, 70-73). It finds the governing file by walking UP from the target through `Path(tgt).resolve()` parents (line 59) looking for `_dont-do.md`/`_dont-do-seeds.md`. But the prohibitions live under `<repo>/my-axon/dev-projects/<slug>/phases/<phase>/_dont-do.md`, while the edited source lives in a disjoint codebase repo (e.g. `/work/myrepo/src/x.py`). The project dir is NOT an ancestor of the codebase file, so the walk-up reaches the codebase repo root / filesystem root and returns None (line 64). dont_do_violation then returns None and the write is allowed. So at write-time too, a tokenized prohibition does nothing for real source edits.
dont_do_enforcement_findings.fix: Resolve the governing _dont-do from the ACTIVE code-dev project (read W:code-dev-project → {proj-dir}/phases/{phase}/_dont-do.md + seeds) whenever the target is inside that project's codebase, instead of relying on a filesystem-ancestor walk.

dont_do_enforcement_findings.title: promote drops the match: token — converts an enforceable prohibition into a prose-only one in seeds (and cascaded phases)
dont_do_enforcement_findings.mechanism: A tokenized prohibition is stored as two lines: `- <text>` then an indented `match: <tok>` (line 70). `promote` appends to seeds only `"- {arg}    _(promoted ...)_\n"` (line 99) — it copies the bullet text `{arg}` but NOT the indented `match:` line, because the match token lives on a separate line and `arg` is just the prohibition text. `--cascade` does the same to every other active phase's `_dont-do.md` (lines 108-109). Result: the promoted/cascaded copy is classified `prose` (no match:, no review:) by the shared parser. That is exactly the fail-closed BLOCK case for R_DONT_DO (r_dont_do.py:125-128), R_DONT_DO_LINT, and Gate 3. So promoting a correctly-captured rule manufactures an un-enforceable rule, and the write-time hook (which does read _dont-do-seeds.md, hook line 60) skips it because it has no token.
dont_do_enforcement_findings.fix: On promote/cascade, carry the source bullet's `match:`/`review:` line too: APPEND `"- {arg} _(promoted...)_\n  match: {match-tok}\n"` (look the token up from the source bullet).

dont_do_enforcement_findings.title: Seeding a new phase from prose-only seeds makes Gate 3 fail-closed BLOCK on a freshly-started phase
dont_do_enforcement_findings.mechanism: phase-start copies `seed-content` (raw seeds bullets) verbatim into the new phase's `_dont-do.md` (lines 50-52). Because promote wrote prose-only bullets into seeds (Bug 3), every newly started phase inherits prose-only prohibitions. Gate 3 lints `{phase-dir}/_dont-do.md` via `dont-do-lint lint` and FAILS fail-closed on any prose-only entry (code-dev-safety-preflight.md:87-89, prose>0 → status fail). So immediately after `code-dev phase start`, preflight is BLOCKED with no code change at fault, and the only escape is to manually re-tokenize each seeded line. Even seeds added by hand per the code-dev-new template guidance ("e.g. 'never commit generated files'", code-dev-new.md:90) are prose and will block.
dont_do_enforcement_findings.fix: Either preserve match:/review: lines through promote→seed (Bug 3) so seeded entries stay enforceable, or have Gate 3 treat newly-seeded-but-untokenized entries as a WARN until first edit instead of a hard BLOCK.
